questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct with regard to the election of the President of India?
I. The voting is conducted through an open ballot.
II. The value of the vote is the same for all the Members of the Parliament.
III. The Election Commission of India is the final adjudicating authority for all election disputes.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation: Article 55 of the Constitution of India describes the manner of election of the President of India. The President is elected by the members of an electoral college consisting of the elected members of both the Houses of Parliament and the elected members of the Legislative Assemblies of States and the Union Territories of Delhi, Puducherry and Jammu and Kashmir. As per the Constitution, the election of the President is held in accordance with the system of proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote and the voting shall be by secret ballot. So, statement I is not correct. Article 55 says that there shall be uniformity in the scale of representation of the different States at the election of the President. Also, there shall be parity between the States as a whole and the Union. For the purpose of securing such uniformity and parity, the number of votes to which each MLA and MP is entitled is determined as follows: Every elected member of the Legislative Assembly of a State shall have as many votes as there are multiples of one thousand in the quotient obtained by dividing the population of the state by the total number of elected members of the Assembly. Each elected member of either House of Parliament shall have such number of votes as may be obtained by dividing the total number of votes assigned to the members of the State Legislation Assemblies by the total number of elected members of both the House of Parliament. Thus, each Member of Parliament (from both Rajya Sabha and the Lok Sabha) has the same value of vote. So, statement II is correct. As per Article 71 of the Constitution, all doubts and disputes relating to the election of a President or Vice-President shall be inquired into and decided by the Supreme Court whose decision shall be final. So, statement III is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of Article 55 and the Presidential election process.

questionText: In a calendar year, how many minimum sessions must take place for the Parliament to fulfil its constitutional responsibility of assembly?
explanation: India does not have a fixed parliamentary calendar. The only constitutional requirement is that there should not be more than six months between two sessions. According to Article 85 of the Constitution of India, the President shall from time to time summon each House of Parliament to meet at such time and place as he thinks fit, but six months shall not intervene between its last sitting in one session and the date appointed for its first sitting in the next session.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of Article 85 and parliamentary sessions.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Lok Sabha Ethics Committee:
I. Its members are appointed by the Speaker for a period of one year.
II. It can take punitive action against the member accused of breach of ethics.
III. It can take up only cases of misconduct that involve MPs.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation: Lok Sabha Ethics Committee is tasked with overseeing the moral and ethical conduct of the members and examining cases referred to it with reference to ethical and other misconduct of members. The ethics committee was constituted in 2000. The members of the Ethics Committee are appointed by the Speaker for a period of one year. So, statement I is correct. In case of rule violations by House officers or employees, the committee can recommend actions such as dismissal of the MP, reprimand, fine, or any other appropriate punishment. The committee only has a recommendatory role and cannot take any punitive action. So, statement II is not correct. The Committee examines complaints filed against members of the House by other members; outsiders through a member; or referred by the Speaker. The term 'unethical' is not defined. It is left to the Committee to decide whether any act is unethical or not. The Ethics Committee can take up only cases of misconduct that involve MPs. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge regarding the Lok Sabha Ethics Committee's composition and powers.

questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct with regard to the Departmentally Related Standing Committees (DRSCs)?
I. The members of DRSCs are elected by the members of the House amongst themselves.
II. All DRSCs have members both from the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha.
III. DRSCs cannot examine the demands for grants of ministries and departments.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation: The Departmentally Related Standing Committees (DRSCs), are constituted to assist Parliament in scrutinising funds allocated to Ministries. At present, there are 24 DRSCs that oversee the working of a Ministry or group of Ministries. Each of them are composed of 31 members: 21 from Lok Sabha and 10 from Rajya Sabha. These DRSCs are constituted for a period of one year. So, statement II is correct. Members of the DRSCs are nominated by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha and the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha, respectively, not directly elected by the entire House. So, statement I is not correct. Key functions of the DRSCs: Examine the Demands for Grants of various ministries and departments and submit a report to the House. So, statement III is not correct. Examining bills referred to them by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha or the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha. Consider annual reports of ministries and departments and review long-term national policy documents.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the composition and functions of DRSCs.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. The Governor of a State has a constitutionally prescribed term of five years.
II. No grounds are mentioned in the Constitution for the removal of the Governor.
III. In case of vacancy, the Chief Justice of the High Court functions as the Governor of the State.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation: The Governor of a State is the executive head of the State appointed by the President at his/her pleasure. Article 156 of the Constitution of India outlines the terms of office for the Governor. As per the article, a term of five years has been prescribed for the Governor-in-office, but the same is subjected to the Doctrine of Pleasure of the President. Although Article 156 prescribes a five-year term, it is subject to the pleasure of the President. So, statement I is correct. The Constitution of India does not mention any specific grounds for the removal of a Governor. So, statement II is correct. Unlike the Office of the President, there is not any similar provision for the office of the Governor. Article 160 empowers the President to make provisions for the Governor's functions when unforeseen circumstances arise that are not specifically covered in the Constitution. Hence, it is up to the President to decide who will take charge of the office in case of a vacancy. So, statement III is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the Governor's term, removal, and vacancy provisions.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
Statement I:
The Governor of a State can reserve a bill for the consideration of the President even without ministerial advice.
Statement II:
Providing assent to bills is a constitutionally recognised discretionary power of the Governor under Article 200.
Statement III:
The decision of the Governor to reserve bills for the President is not subject to Judicial Review.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
options: (a) Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I
(b) Both Statement II and Statement III are correct, but only one of them explains Statement I
(c) Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement–I
(d) Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct
explanation: Under Article 200 of the Constitution of India, when a Bill passed by the State Legislature is presented, the Governor may assent to the Bill, withhold assent, reserve it for the President's consideration, or return it (except a Money Bill) for reconsideration. The Governor is generally bound by the advice of the Council of Ministers, as per Article 163, and must act accordingly under Article 200 except in exceptional and narrowly defined circumstances where the Governor can use the discretion. In these cases, the Governor may reserve the Bill for the President even without ministerial advice. So, statement I is correct. However, the power to grant or withhold assent is not an unfettered discretionary power. As clarified by the Supreme Court in State of Tamil Nadu v Governor of Tamil Nadu, 2025 withholding assent is not a constitutionally recognised discretionary power, except in exceptional cases of constitutional concern. So, statement II is not correct. In the same case the Court further held that the decision to reserve a Bill for the President is subject to judicial review. So, statement III is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the Governor's discretionary powers and recent Supreme Court judgments.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. A person must be qualified for election as a member of the Lok Sabha to be eligible for the office of the President and the Vice-President of India.
II. The President of India may tender his/her resignation to the Vice-President and vice versa.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Article 58 provides that no person shall be eligible for election as President unless he/she is a citizen of India, has completed the age of thirty-five years, and is qualified for election as a member of the House of the People (Lok Sabha). Article 66 says that no person shall be eligible for election as Vice-President (VP) unless he is a citizen of India; has completed the age of thirty-five years; and is qualified for election as a member of the Council of States (Rajya Sabha). While the first two eligibility conditions are same for both the offices, for the President a person needs to be eligible for being a member of Lok Sabha, while for VP's office one must be eligible for the membership of Rajya Sabha. So, statement I is not correct. According to Article 56, the President may address his/her resignation to the Vice-President. Under Article 67, the Vice-President may address his resignation to the President. So, statement II is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the eligibility and resignation provisions for the President and Vice-President.

questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Parliamentary Device Feature
I. Calling Attention Motion Not subject to the vote of the House
II. Adjournment Motion Has an element of censure against the government
III. Censure Motion If passed, the Minister must resign
IV. Zero Hour Informal device for members to raise important matters
V. Special Mention Usually raised by an opposition member in order to control the government
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
explanation: Calling Attention: A Member may, with the prior permission of the Speaker, call the attention of a Minister to any matter of urgent public importance and the Minister may make a brief statement thereon. The Calling Attention matter is not subject to the vote of the House. So, pair I is correctly matched. Adjournment Motion: It is the procedure for adjournment of the business of the House for the purpose of discussing a definite matter of urgent public importance. Its adoption is regarded as a sort of censure of the Government. So, pair II is correctly matched. Censure Motion: It is moved against the Government censuring its policy in some direction or an individual minister or ministers of the Government. If it is passed in the Lok Sabha, the ministers need not resign from the office. So, pair III is not correctly matched. Zero Hour: The time immediately following the Question Hour and laying of papers and before any listed business is taken up in the House. It is an informal device available to the members of the Parliament to raise matters. So, pair IV is correctly matched. Special Mention: A matter which is not a point of order or which cannot be raised during question hour, half-an hour discussion, short duration discussion or under adjournment motion, calling attention notice or under any rule of the House can be raised under the special mention in the Rajya Sabha. Its equivalent procedural device in the Lok Sabha is known as 'Notice (Mention) Under Rule 377'. So, pair V is not correctly matched.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of various parliamentary motions and their implications.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Privileges of the Parliament:
I. The Constitution of India provides for two privileges, freedom of speech in Parliament and the Right of Publication of its proceedings.
II. The Presiding Officer of the House is the sole arbitrator to adjudge what constitutes the privilege of the Parliament.
III. The Parliament can punish both the members and outsiders for the breach of its privilege.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation: Parliamentary privileges are special rights and immunities enjoyed by the Houses and their members to ensure independent functioning. Article 105 of the Constitution of India provides for the powers, privileges, and immunities of the Houses of Parliament, their members, and committees. The Constitution of India explicitly mentioned two privileges, that is, Freedom of Speech in Parliament and the Right of Publication of its proceedings. So, statement I is correct. In the Sita Soren vs Union Of India, 2024 case the Supreme Court said that the Parliament is not the sole judge of privileges it enjoys and the court can adjudicate on the matter. So, statement II is not correct. Parliament holds quasi-judicial powers in matters related to its privileges, allowing it to act against any individual member or non-member who obstructs or disrespects its functioning. Parliament can impose punishments ranging from reprimand to imprisonment depending on the severity. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of parliamentary privileges and recent judicial interpretations.

questionText:
Consider the following:
I. Controller General of Accounts
II. National Council for Transgender Persons
III. NITI Aayog
IV. Central Vigilance Commission
How many of the above are statutory bodies/ offices?
explanation: Controller General of Accounts (CGA): It serves as the Principal Advisor on accounting matters to the Union Government. It functions under the Department of Expenditure, Ministry of Finance. It is a non-statutory office. So, point I is not correct. National Council for Transgender Persons (NCTP): It is a statutory body constituted under the Transgender Persons (Protection of Rights) Act 2019 to safeguard the rights of transgender persons in India. So, point II is correct. National Institution for Transforming India (NITI Aayog): It was established in 2015, replacing the erstwhile Planning Commission. It is a non-constitutional non-statutory body, created through an executive resolution of the Union Government. So, point III is not correct. Central Vigilance Commission (CVC): It is the premier Governmental agency for preventing corruption in the Central Government. Originally the CVC was neither a constitutional body nor a statutory body. Later, in 2003, the Parliament enacted a law conferring statutory status on the CVC through the Central Vigilance Commission Act, 2003. So, point IV is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the statutory or non-statutory status of various government bodies.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Lok Sabha:
I. A member can be suspended from the Lok Sabha only after a motion is passed in the House to that effect.
II. Once suspended the suspension of a member can be revoked at will by the Speaker.
III. The Speaker can ask for any member to withdraw from the House.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Speaker of the Lok Sabha and the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha are responsible for maintaining order in Parliament. For suspension of members in Lok Sabha, the speaker under Rule 374 of the 'Rules and procedure of Lok Sabha' can name a member and present a motion for the suspension of the particular member or members. The motion on being passed by the House, results in the suspension of the member. The authority to revoke that suspension lies with the House. In such cases, the House can revoke the suspension by passing a resolution through a motion. So, statement II is not correct. In 2001, the Lok Sabha rules were amended to give the speaker one additional power. A new Rule 374A was added, which allowed for automatic suspension (without a motion) of a member named by the Speaker for grave disorder, for 5 sittings or the rest of the session, whichever is less. Hence, the Lok Sabha speaker does not have to necessarily put forth a motion to suspend a member. So, statement I is not correct. The Rule 373 of the Rules of procedure gives power to the Speaker for directing a member to withdraw immediately for grossly disorderly conduct. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Detailed knowledge of the rules of procedure regarding the suspension of MPs in the Lok Sabha.

questionText:
Which of the following parliamentary devices can be introduced only in Lok Sabha?
I. Censure motion
II. Adjournment Motion
III. Privilege Motion
IV. No Confidence Motion
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation: Censure Motion: It is a formal resolution moved by the Opposition in the Lok Sabha to express strong disapproval of the policies or actions of the Council of Ministers or an individual Minister. Censure Motion can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha, as the Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lower House under Article 75(3) of the Constitution. So, point I is correct. Adjournment Motion: It is a parliamentary device used to draw attention to a specific, urgent, recent matter of public importance by interrupting the normal business of the House. It is allowed only in Lok Sabha and requires the support of at least 50 members to be admitted. So, point II is correct. No-Confidence Motion: It is a motion that tests whether the Council of Ministers continues to enjoy the confidence of the Lok Sabha. The motion can be introduced only in Lok Sabha and requires the support of at least 50 members to be admitted. If passed by a simple majority, the entire Council of Ministers must resign. So, point IV is correct. Privilege Motion: It is moved when there is a breach of privilege or contempt of the House or its members. Parliamentary privileges are granted under Article 105 to both Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha. Hence, privilege motions can be initiated in either House. So, point III is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of various parliamentary motions and the Houses in which they can be introduced.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. The Constitution of India does not specify any time limit for the President to give his/ her assent to a bill.
II. The President of India does not enjoy absolute veto over the bills passed by the Parliament.
III. The President of India does not have the power of a qualified Veto.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Article 111 of the Constitution of India says that 'When a Bill has been passed by the Houses of Parliament, it shall be presented to the President and the President shall declare either that he assents to the Bill, or that he withholds assent.' The Constitution does not specify any specified time limit for the President to approve a Bill. So, statement I is correct. Absolute Veto refers to the power of the President to withhold his assent to a bill passed by the Parliament. The bill then ends and does not become an act. So, statement II is not correct. Qualified veto refers to the veto powers which can be overridden and conditional, in this kind of veto, the condition for the sustenance is that if the bill which is vetoed by the President gets passed from the Legislature with a higher majority than it had for in the earlier instance, the veto gets overridden. The President of India, doesn't have any qualified veto. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the President's veto powers and Article 111.

questionText:
Consider the following:
I. Fixed tenure of six years or up to the age of 65 years
II. Removal only in the manner of the judge of the Supreme Court
III. Not eligible for reappointment in the Central or the State Government
IV. Salary equivalent to the judge of the Supreme Court
How many of the above provisions are made in the Constitution of India for the office of the Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG)?
explanation: Article 148 of the Constitution of India provides for an independent office of the Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG). Article 148 states that, the CAG shall be appointed by the President by warrant under his hand and seal and shall only be removed from office in like manner and on like grounds as a Judge of the Supreme Court. So, point II is correct. The CAG shall not be eligible for further office either under the Government of India or under the Government of any State after he has ceased to hold his office. So, point III is correct. The Act states that, the CAG shall hold office for a term of six years from the date on which he assumes such office or till he attains the age of sixty-five years, whichever is earlier. So, point I is not correct. Also, Article 148 states that, the salary and other conditions of service of the CAG shall be such as may be determined by Parliament by law and, until they are so determined, shall be as specified in the Second Schedule: As per the CAG (DPC) Act, 1971 the salary of the CAG is equal to the salary of the Judge of the Supreme Court. So, point IV is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the constitutional provisions and statutory rules governing the CAG.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Anti Defection Law:
I. The Presiding Officer of the House can take suo moto cognisance of the defection.
II. There is no time limit for the presiding officer to decide on the disqualification proceedings as per Schedule X.
III. In the Kihoto Hollohan case (1992), the Supreme Court held that the decision of the Presiding Officer regarding disqualification is subject to judicial review.
Which of the statements given above is/are not correct?
explanation: The 52nd Constitutional Amendment Act of 1985 introduced the anti-defection law by adding the Tenth Schedule to the Constitution. The anti-defection law allows any member of the House to initiate the process of disqualification by filing a petition/complaint with the Speaker (Lok Sabha) or Chairman (Rajya Sabha) alleging defection by another member. The Presiding Officer of the House cannot take suo moto cognisance of the defection. So, statement I is not correct. The Speaker of Lok Sabha, the Chairman of Rajya Sabha or the State Legislative Assembly is the deciding authority who decides disqualification petitions under the anti-defection law. However, the law does not specify a strict timeline for the decision. The Presiding Officers can decide on the case without any time limits. So, statement II is correct. The Supreme Court in Kihoto Hollohan vs. Zachillhu and Others (1992) said that the decisions of the Speaker or Chairman regarding disqualification are subject to judicial review to ensure a system of checks and balances. However, judicial intervention is limited to cases involving malafide intention, perversity, or violation of natural justice. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the Anti-Defection Law and related Supreme Court judgments.

questionText: For the purpose of Article 83 of the Constitution of India, which one of the following is taken as the initiation date of the newly constituted Lok Sabha?
options: (a) Day of the result declaration for general election
(b) Day of the oath taking of the Council of Ministers
(c) Day of the first sitting of the Lok Sabha
(d) Day of the assumption of office by the Speaker
explanation: Article 83(2) of the Constitution of India and Section 14 of the Representation of the People Act, 1951 provides for the conduct of the elections to constitute a new Lok Sabha before the expiry of its current term. For the purpose of calculating the five year period, it is the date of the first sitting of the Lok Sabha which is taken to be the initiation date. For example, for the 17th Lok Sabha the first sitting took place on 17th June 2019, therefore its term expired on 16th June 2024, and elections for the 18th Lok Sabha were concluded on 4th June 2024.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of how the term of the Lok Sabha is calculated.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to election petitions in India:
I. The High Courts have original jurisdiction on deciding the election petition.
II. They have to be submitted within thirty days of the declaration of result.
III. They need to be mandatorily disposed of within one year of its filing.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Election petition is a procedure for inquiring into the validity of the election results of Parliamentary or local government elections. Election petitions are filed in the High Court of the particular state in which the election was conducted. Therefore, only the High Courts have the original jurisdiction on deciding on election petitions. So, statement I is correct. Under Section 81(1) of the RPA, 1951, an election petition must be filed within 45 days from the date of the declaration of the result. If the petition is submitted even one day after the 45-day window, it is liable to be rejected as barred by limitation. So, statement II is not correct. Sub-section (7) of section 86 of the Representation of the People Act (RPA), 1951 provides that every election petition shall be tried as expeditiously as possible and endeavour shall be made to conclude the trial within six months from the date on which the election petition is presented to the High Court for trial. So, statement III is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the Representation of the People Act, 1951 regarding election petitions.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Right to Information (RTI) Act, 2005:
I. It has an overriding effect over the provisions of the Official Secrets Act, 1923.
II. It is applicable to all constitutional authorities except the judiciary.
III. Personal details of officials can be accessed under the RTI in public interest.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: The Right to Information (RTI) Act, 2005 mandates timely response to citizen requests for government information. Section 22 of the RTI Act provides for the primacy of the RTI Act vis-a-vis provisions of other laws, including OSA. This gives the RTI Act an overriding effect, notwithstanding anything inconsistent with the provisions of OSA. So, if there is any inconsistency in OSA with regard to furnishing of information, it will be superseded by the RTI Act. So, statement I is correct. The RTI Act is applicable to all constitutional authorities, including the executive, legislature and judiciary; any institution or body established or constituted by an Act of Parliament or a State Legislature. So, statement II is not correct. Under the RTI Act, it is not mandatory for the public authorities to disclose information, which relates to personal information, the disclosure of which has no relationship to any public activity or interest, or which would cause unwarranted invasion of the privacy of the individual. The Supreme Court of India in the matter of Girish R. Deshpande vs. CIC and others had ruled that the personal information of government officials could be disclosed, only if it would serve a larger public interest. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the RTI Act, its overriding effect, and exemptions.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Central Vigilance Commission (CVC):
I. It advises the Central Government on the appointment of the Director of Prosecution in the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI).
II. The Chief Vigilance Commissioner is a member of the committee that recommends candidates for appointment as the Director of the CBI.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Central Vigilance Commission (CVC) is the main agency for preventing corruption in the Central government. The Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013 has amended some provisions of the CVC Act, 2003. The Act mandates that appointment of Director of Prosecution by the Central Government under the Directorate of Prosecution in Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) to be done on the recommendation of the CVC. So, statement I is correct. The CBI director is appointed according to provisions under the Delhi Special Police Establishment Act (1946) and amendments therein brought by the Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013. The appointment is made by the Centre on recommendation by a three-member Selection Committee composed of the Prime Minister as the chairperson, the Leader of the Opposition (LoP) in the Lok Sabha and the Chief Justice of India, or a Supreme Court judge nominated by him. So, statement II is not correct. CBI is an elite force playing a major role in preservation of values in public life and in ensuring the health of the national economy. It is also the nodal police agency in India, which coordinates investigations on behalf of Interpol Member countries. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the CVC and the appointment process of the CBI Director.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
Statement I:
The process of impeachment of the President of India is a quasi-judicial procedure.
Statement II:
The Members of the concerned House of the Parliament act as the investigating agency during the impeachment process of the President.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
options: (a) Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
(b) Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
(c) Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
(d) Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
explanation: The President can be removed from office by a process of impeachment for 'violation of the Constitution'. Article 61 explicitly uses the term impeachment and says that 'When a President is to be impeached for violation of the Constitution, the charge shall be preferred by either House of Parliament.' The impeachment charges on the President can be initiated through a resolution in either House of Parliament. When such a resolution has been passed by the House, it is referred to another House. The other House investigates the charges and then decides whether the charges preferred against the President have been sustained or not. So, statement II is correct. A quasi-judicial procedure is one in which the Members of the House are acting as judges rather than legislators; the House is not making new laws, but rather applying existing laws to specific facts. So, the impeachment process involves investigation, presentation of the evidence, hearing the parties and making a decision similar to the judicial process. So, statement I is correct. Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the impeachment process of the President and its quasi-judicial nature.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI):
I. It functions under the Department of Personnel and Training.
II. The Director of CBI enjoys security of tenure.
III. It coordinates investigations on behalf of Interpol Member countries.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: The Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI), functioning under the Department of Personnel, Ministry of Personnel, Pension & Public Grievances, Government of India, is the premier investigating police agency in India. It is a non-statutory executive body, established under the Delhi Police Special Establishment Act, 1946. So, statement I is correct. The Director of the CBI is appointed by the Government on the basis of recommendations from a collegium consisting of the Prime Minister, Leader of Opposition in Lok Sabha, Chief Justice of India or any Supreme Court judge nominated by him/her. The Director of CBI was provided a secured tenure of two years via the Central Vigilance Commission Act, 2003. This was post the verdict of the Supreme Court in the Vineet Narain versus Union of India case, 1997 wherein the court issued a specific order that the CBI director shall have a minimum tenure of two years. So, statement II is correct. CBI is an elite force playing a major role in preservation of values in public life and in ensuring the health of the national economy. It is also the nodal police agency in India, which coordinates investigations on behalf of Interpol Member countries. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the CBI's establishment, tenure of its Director, and its role.

questionText:
Which of the following statements are correct with regard to the Chief Election Commissioner (CEC) of India?
I. The Prime Minister, the Leader of the Opposition in the Lok Sabha, and the Chief Justice of India constitute members of the panel for his selection.
II. S/he is removed by following the same procedure as the removal of the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha.
Select the correct answer using the code below:
explanation: The Chief Election Commissioner (CEC) of India plays a crucial role in ensuring free and fair elections. Appointed by the President under Article 324(2) of the Constitution. The Election Commissioners (Appointment, Conditions of Service and Term of Office) Act, 2023 regulates the appointment of the CEC and the Election Commissioners (ECs). Under the amendment, the selection panel consists of the Prime Minister, the Leader of the Opposition in the Lok Sabha, and the Union Cabinet Minister nominated by the PM. So, statement I is not correct. CEC cannot be removed from office except in the manner and on the grounds prescribed for a Supreme Court judge by a resolution passed by both Houses of Parliament with a special majority, and on the grounds of proved misbehaviour or incapacity. So, statement II is not correct. However, the other Election Commissioners (ECs), unlike the CEC, do not enjoy the same level of protection. They can be removed on the recommendation of the CEC.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the recent changes in the appointment process of the CEC and ECs, and their removal procedures.

questionText:
Consider the following constitutional functionaries:
I. Chief Election Commissioner
II. Comptroller and Auditor General
III. Attorney General
IV. Chairman of the National Commission of Scheduled Castes
How many of the above can be removed on the grounds of proved misbehaviour or incapacity?
explanation: Article 324(5) of the Constitution of India states that the Chief Election Commissioner can only be removed in the same manner and on the same grounds as a Supreme Court judge, that is for proved misbehaviour or incapacity. So, point I is correct. According to Article 148(1) of the Constitution of India, the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) can be removed from office by the President, but on the grounds of proved misbehaviour or incapacity, and following the same procedure as that for removing a Supreme Court judge. So, point II is correct. Article 76 of the Constitution of India establishes the office of the Attorney General (AG) and outlines the President's power to appoint them, but it does not specify the grounds or procedure for their removal. The Constitution states that the AG holds office during the pleasure of the President. So, point IV is not correct. The National Commission for Scheduled Castes (NCSC) is a constitutional body under Article 338. While the Constitution outlines its establishment and functions, the specific grounds for removing its chairman and the process involved are not detailed in the Constitution itself. So, point V is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the removal grounds for various constitutional functionaries.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Union Public Service Commission (UPSC):
I. The Constitution does not prescribe a fixed strength for the Commission.
II. At least half of the UPSC members must have held office for a minimum of ten years under either the Government of India or a State Government.
III. The Chairman of the UPSC is not eligible for any further employment under the Government of India or a State Government.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Union Public Service Commission (UPSC) is a Constitutional body established under Article 315 of the Constitution of India. The UPSC comprises a Chairman and other members who are appointed by the President of India. The Constitution does not prescribe a fixed strength for the Commission, leaving its composition to be determined at the President's discretion. So, statement I is correct. The Constitution lays down singular qualifications for members of the UPSC that at least half of the members must have held office for a minimum of ten years under either the Government of India or a State Government. So, statement II is correct. On ceasing to hold office, the Chairman of the UPSC is not eligible for any further employment under the Government of India or a State Government. A member of the UPSC, however, may be appointed as the Chairman of the UPSC or as the Chairman of a State Public Service Commission, but is not eligible for any other government employment at the Centre or in the States, hence the members of UPSC cannot be reappointed for the same post the expiry of their term. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the composition, qualifications, and post-retirement restrictions of UPSC members.

questionText: In a situation when the offices of both the President and the Vice President are vacant at the same time, then as per the Constitution of India, who amongst the following will discharge the functions of the President of India?
options: (b) The Speaker of the Lok Sabha
(c) The Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha
explanation: Article 65 in the Constitution of India makes Vice-President to act as President or to discharge his functions during casual vacancies in the office, or during the absence of the President. However, the Constitution makes no provisions regarding who will act as the President of India when the posts of President as well as Vice-President fall vacant at the same time. This exigency has been dealt with by the President (Discharge of Functions) Act, 1969 which provides that in case the offices of both the President and Vice-President falls vacant, the Chief Justice of India or, in his absence, the senior-most Judge of the Supreme Court of India available shall discharge the functions of the President until a new President is elected. Mohammad Hidayatullah is the only Chief Justice of India who acted as a President of India when President Dr. Zakir Hussain died and then Vice-President V.V. Giri resigned from his post to contest the Presidential election prior to the 1969 presidential election.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the succession line for the President's office and historical instances.

questionText:
With regard to the Lok Sabha, consider the following statements:
I. All the members of the Lok Sabha are directly elected on the basis of Universal Adult Suffrage.
II. Fourth Schedule of the Constitution provides for the allocation of Lok Sabha seats among the States.
III. The Constitution (106th Amendment) Act of 2023, reserved one-third of all seats for women in Lok Sabha.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Lok Sabha is the Lower House of the Indian Parliament. Presently all the 543 members of the Lok Sabha are elected directly by the people, making it a completely elected House. Earlier it was a partly elected and partly nominated body (prior to 104th Constitutional Amendment in 2019, the Lok Sabha also had provisions for nominated members, the President could nominate two members of Anglo-Indian community). So, statement I is correct. Fourth Schedule of the Constitution provides for allocation of seats to States in the Council of States (Rajya Sabha). So, statement II is not correct. The Constitution (106th Amendment) Act of 2023, reserves one-third of all seats for women in Lok Sabha, State legislative assemblies, and the Legislative Assembly of the National Capital Territory of Delhi. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the composition of the Lok Sabha, relevant schedules, and recent constitutional amendments.

questionText:
Consider the following situations:
I. If he/she commits a breach of a parliamentary privilege
II. If he/she is elected to the State Legislative Assembly
III. If he/she is convicted of a criminal offence with imprisonment for more than two years
IV. If he/she disobeys a three-line whip issued by his political party
In how many of the above situations does a Member of Parliament get disqualified immediately from his/her seat in the Parliament?
explanation: If any individual or authority violates or disregards any of the privileges, powers and immunities of the House or members or committees thereof, he may be punished for 'breach of privilege.' But any of this punishment will ensue only after the charges against the member are proved in a quasi-judicial inquiry of the House or any of its committee, further for any such punishment a motion needs to be passed by the House. So, point I is not correct. According to Article 101 of the Constitution, a person cannot be a member of both the Parliament and the State Legislature at the same time. If a person is so elected, his seat in Parliament becomes vacant if he/she does not resign his seat in the State Legislature within 14 days. So, point II is not correct. Under the Representation of People Act (1951), a person shall be disqualified for being elected as a member of Parliament if he has been convicted for any offence resulting in imprisonment for two or more years. In such a scenario the court order for conviction is deemed as the disqualification order for the particular legislator, also there is no need for the official notification from the Parliamentary Secretariat for the same. So, point III is correct. The Anti Defection Law says that a member of a House belonging to any political party shall be disqualified for being a member of the House if he votes or abstains from voting contrary to direction issued by his political party. However, the disqualification is not immediate. If the party concerned condones the action of its legislator within fifteen days of any such actions, no disqualification proceedings are initiated. So, point IV is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the various grounds and procedures for the disqualification of MPs.

questionText:
With regard to the Speaker of Lok Sabha, consider the following statements:
I. She/he holds the office during the pleasure of the President.
II. She/he cannot vote on any motion or resolution in the Lok Sabha.
III. Only she/he can prorogue a session of the Parliament.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: As per Article 93 of the Constitution of India, the members of the Lok Sabha may elect the Speaker and the Deputy Speaker from among its members. Usually, the Speaker remains in office during the life of the Lok Sabha. The Speaker may be removed by a resolution passed by a majority of all the then members of the Lok Sabha. Such a resolution can be moved only after giving 14 days' advance notice. His tenure does not depend on the pleasure of the President. So, statement I is not correct. The Speaker is allowed to give his/her casting vote. According to Article 100 of the Constitution, which talks about voting in the Houses, the Chairman of Rajya Sabha or Speaker of Lok Sabha, or any person acting as such, 'shall not vote in the first instance, but shall exercise a casting vote in the case of any equality of votes'. So, statement II is not correct. Termination of a session of the House by an order of the President is called 'prorogation'. Under Article 85(2) of the Constitution, the President may from time to time prorogue sessions in either House of Parliament. So, statement III is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the election, removal, voting rights of the Speaker, and prorogation of the House.

questionText:
With reference to the Union Government, consider the following pairs:
Cabinet Committee Chairperson
I. Cabinet Committee on Accommodation Prime Minister
II. Cabinet Committee on Political Affairs Home Minister
III. Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs Finance Minister
IV. Cabinet Committee on Security Defence Minister
Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
explanation: The Cabinet Committees are set up by the Prime Minister with selected members of the Cabinet. Cabinet Committee on Accommodation: It is a specialized committee within the Indian government responsible for overseeing matters related to the allocation of government accommodation. This committee is chaired by the Union Home Minister. So, pair I is not correctly matched. Cabinet Committee on Political Affairs: It handles issues involving relations between the national and state governments, examines economic and societal matters requiring a comprehensive view, and addresses international matters lacking internal or external security repercussions. Known also as the 'super cabinet,' its assemblage incorporates most of the principal Cabinet ministers. This committee is chaired by the Prime Minister himself. So, pair II is not correctly matched. Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs: It is a key decision-making body within the Indian government, primarily responsible for handling economic policy matters. This committee is chaired by the Prime Minister himself. So, pair III is not correctly matched. Cabinet Committee on Security: It is led by the Prime Minister and includes the Ministers of Defence, Home Affairs, Finance, and External Affairs. Its key responsibilities include shaping national security and defense policies, overseeing defense procurement, and guiding the country's strategic posture, particularly in nuclear and missile programs. So, pair IV is not correctly matched.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the various Cabinet Committees and their chairpersons.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Attorney General (AG) for India:
I. He must be eligible to be appointed as a judge of the Supreme Court.
II. He enjoys all the privileges and immunities that are available to a Member of Parliament.
III. He is not prohibited from taking up private cases during his term of office.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: Article 76 of the Constitution of India has provided for the office of the Attorney General for India. He is the highest law officer in the country. The Attorney General (AG) is appointed by the President. Any person who is as eligible to become a judge of the Supreme Court can be appointed as the AG. So, statement I is correct. He enjoys all the privileges and immunities that are available to a member of Parliament. Further, he has the right to speak and to take part in the proceedings of both the Houses of Parliament or their joint sitting and any committee of the Parliament of which he may be named a member, but without a right to vote. So, statement II is correct. The AG is not a fulltime counsel for the Government. He does not fall in the category of government servants. Further, he is not barred from private legal practice during his term of office. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the appointment, privileges, and restrictions of the Attorney General for India.

questionText:
With reference to the enactment of the Union Budget, which of the following powers are not granted to the Rajya Sabha?
I. General discussion on the budget
II. Voting on demand for grants
III. Amendment to the appropriation bill
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation: The Union Budget in India is a constitutional and procedural exercise carried out annually by the central government. General Discussion: The Chairman appoints the day and time for general discussion on the Budget in Rajya Sabha. The House is at liberty to discuss the Budget as a whole or any question of principle involved therein, but no motion is moved nor the Budget is submitted to the vote of Rajya Sabha. So, point I is not correct. Voting on Demands for Grants: Voting on Demands for Grants is exclusive to the Lok Sabha; the Rajya Sabha has no voting powers in this matter. Based on the reports of the Departmental Standing Committees, the Lok Sabha proceeds to vote on the Demands for Grants during the budget process. A Demand for Grant becomes a Grant only after it is duly voted upon by the Lok Sabha. So, point II is correct. Passing of Appropriation Bill: Once the Demands for Grants are passed by the Lok Sabha, they are consolidated into the Appropriation Bill. Passing the bill provides legal authority to the government to spend money from the Consolidated Fund. After being passed by the Lok Sabha, the bill is transmitted to the Rajya Sabha for their consideration. The Rajya Sabha has no power to amend or reject it. It can only recommend changes, and must return the bill within 14 days. So, point III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the stages of the Union Budget and the respective powers of the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
Statement I:
No person who is in police custody at the time of elections can contest elections.
Statement II:
No person who is in police custody at the time of elections is allowed to vote.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
options: (a) Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
(b) Both Statement I and Statement II are correct, but Statement II does not explain Statement I
(c) Statement I is correct, but Statement II is not correct
(d) Statement I is not correct, but Statement II is correct
explanation: Under the Representation of the People Act, if a person is convicted and sentenced to a jail term of not less than two years, he/she stands disqualified automatically from contesting elections for six years from the date of release from prison. The bar on contesting elections applies only after a person has been convicted. Persons who are under police custody during the time of elections can contest elections. So, statement I is not correct. According to Section 62(5) of the RP Act, a person who is in jail or in police custody is not permitted to vote in any election. This restriction extends to individuals who are serving a sentence after conviction, awaiting trial as an undertrial, or held in police custody even temporarily (such as during remand), or detained for any other reason. So, statement II is correct. However, this restriction does not apply to a person under preventive detention (as per the proviso to Section 62(5)).
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the Representation of the People Act regarding disqualification and voting rights of persons in custody.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the None of The Above (NOTA) option available in the elections:
I. If the NOTA receives the highest number of votes than any of the contesting candidates then a re-election needs to be conducted.
II. NOTA votes are not counted for the calculation of total votes for the return of security deposits to candidates.
III. There is no provision of NOTA option in the Rajya Sabha elections.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation: None of The Above (NOTA) is an option on the Electronic Voting Machine (EVM) or ballot that allows voters to reject all candidates contesting in an election. As per the rules framed by the Election Commission of India, the votes polled under NOTA are part of the total votes polled in an election, but are not calculated under valid votes polled, and for the result calculation purposes the latter parameter is taken as the consideration. That is why even if NOTA receives the highest number of votes, but a contesting candidate receives the second-highest, that candidate can still be declared the winner. This is considered as the major disadvantage of NOTA. So, statement I is not correct. According to Section 158 of the Representation of the People Act, 1951, only the valid votes received by the contesting candidates are counted to determine if a candidate has secured enough votes to retain their deposit. A candidate must secure at least one-sixth of the valid votes to retain their deposit. So, statement II is correct. In 2018, the Supreme Court scrapped the use of NOTA option in the Rajya Sabha elections. The court held that the Rajya Sabha elections are indirect and follow proportional representation through a single transferable vote under Article 80(4). Since MLAs vote via an open ballot to maintain party discipline, introducing NOTA would violate this system and go against the Supreme Court's 2013 PUCL judgment, which allows NOTA only in direct elections. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of NOTA, its implications on election results, and its applicability in different types of elections.

questionText:
Which of the following crimes come under the jurisdiction of the National Investigation Agency?
I. Human trafficking
II. Counterfeit currency
III. Cyberterrorism
IV. Sale of prohibited arms
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation: The National Investigation Agency (NIA) was set up under the National Investigation Agency Act, 2008 as a Central Counter Terrorism Law Enforcement Agency. The 2008 Act provided jurisdiction to NIA over the Atomic Energy Act, 1962, and the Unlawful Activities Prevention Act, 1967. Later, an amendment in 2019 extended its jurisdiction over the offences of: Human trafficking. So, point I is correct. Counterfeit currency or bank notes. So, point II is correct. Cyber-terrorism. So, point III is correct. Manufacture or sale of prohibited arms. So, point IV is correct. Offences under the Explosive Substances Act, 1908.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the expanded jurisdiction of the NIA following the 2019 amendment.

questionText:
Consider the following:
I. Withholding assent to a State Bill which was reserved for her consideration
II. Consulting the Supreme Court of India under Article 143 of the Constitution
III. Appointment of Prime Minister when no party has a clear majority in the Lok Sabha
In how many of the above situations can the President of India use her discretion?
explanation: As per Article 53 of the Constitution of India, the executive authority of the Union is vested with the President, when read with Article 74 of the Constitution, these powers are to be exercised by the Presidential office in aid and advice of the Council of Ministers headed by the Prime Minister. In essence the President, as per the Constitution of India, doesn't have any power which he/she can discharge as per its discretion, the advice of the Council of Ministers is paramount for the President to function. A bill passed by a State Legislature may also be reserved for the consideration of the President by the Governor of that State (under Article 200). The President enjoys veto power in relation to such a bill passed by a State Legislature. However, he/she exercises this power only on the aid and advice of the Union Council of Ministers. So, point I is not correct. Article 143 empowers the President to consult the Supreme Court. The President makes such a reference based on the advice of the Union council of ministers. It is not a discretionary power. So, point II is not correct. Situational discretion means those discretionary powers that are derived from the exigencies of a prevailing political situation. The President has certain situational discretion: Appointment of Prime Minister when no party has a clear majority in the Lok Sabha or when the Prime Minister in office dies suddenly and there is no obvious successor. So, point III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the President's discretionary powers versus powers exercised on the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
Statement I:
The Prime Minister is considered to be the Keystone of the Cabinet Arch in India.
Statement II:
The resignation of the Prime Minister automatically dissolves the Council of Ministers.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
options: (a) Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
(b) Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
(c) Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
(d) Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
explanation: In India's parliamentary government, the President is the nominal executive while the Prime Minister (and the Council of Ministers) is the real executive. Dr. BR Ambedkar in the Constituent Assembly made a comment while discussing the office of the Prime Minister that the Prime Minister is really the keystone of the arch of the Cabinet. In the Westminster model of parliamentary governance (as is practiced in India), it is said that the Prime Minister is central to the formation of the Cabinet, central to its life and central to its death. The Government is the master of the country and the Prime Minister is the master of the government. So, statement I is correct. The Prime Minister stands at the head of the Council of Ministers, the other ministers cannot function when the Prime Minister resigns or dies. In other words, the resignation or death of an incumbent Prime Minister automatically dissolves the Council of Ministers and thereby generates a vacuum. The Prime Minister is thus the real maker and controller of the Council of Ministers. So, statement II is correct. Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the Prime Minister's central role in the Cabinet and the principle of collective responsibility.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Central Information Commission (CIC):
I. It can consist of a maximum of 3 members.
II. It can not take any action suo moto against an official.
III. It submits its annual report to the President.
Which of the statements given above are not correct?
explanation: The Central Information Commission (CIC) is a statutory body under the provisions of the Right to Information Act (2005). The Commission consists of a Chief Information Commissioner and not more than ten Information Commissioners. Hence, it can consist of a maximum of 11 members. So, statement I is not correct. It can also initiate inquiries on its own (suo motu) if there are reasonable grounds to do so. While conducting these inquiries, the Commission holds the authority of a civil court. So, statement II is not correct. The CIC is responsible for preparing an annual report that details the implementation of the provisions outlined in the Right to Information Act. The report is submitted to the Central Government, which is then presented before each House of Parliament. So, statement III is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the composition, powers, and reporting mechanism of the Central Information Commission.

questionText:
With regard to the Union Executive, which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Parliament.
II. The Ministers are individually responsible to the President of India.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation: The fundamental principle underlying the working of the Parliamentary system of Government is this principle of Executive responsibility. In Article 75 of the Constitution of India, the concept of both individual responsibility and collective responsibility are mentioned. Article 75 (3) in the Constitution of India says that the Council of Ministers shall be collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha. This means that all the ministers own joint responsibility to the Lok Sabha for all their acts of omission and commission. So, statement I is not correct. Article 75 also says that the Ministers shall hold office during the pleasure of the President. It means that the Members of the Council of Ministers are individually responsible to the President of India. This means that the President can remove a Minister even at a time when the Council of Ministers enjoys the confidence of the Lok Sabha. However, the President removes a minister only on the advice of the Prime Minister. Thus, in practice, the Ministers are responsible to the Prime Minister. So, statement II is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of collective and individual responsibility of Ministers under Article 75.

questionText:
Consider the following situations:
I. A member resigns from the party as he is elected to the post of Speaker
II. A member voluntarily resigns from the party, if his party in opposition joins the treasury benches
III. An independent member joins the ruling party
IV. If a nominated member joins any political party within one month of his nomination
How many of the above situations will attract proceedings under the Tenth Schedule of the Constitution?
explanation: The Tenth Schedule of the Constitution of India, lays down the conditions under which legislators may be disqualified on grounds of defection. Grounds for Disqualification: Voluntary Resignation from Party Membership: A member can be disqualified if they 'voluntarily' give up their membership of the political party to which they belong regardless of whether the party moves from opposition to treasury. So, point II is correct. An independent member becomes disqualified if he joins any political party after the election. So, point III is correct. Exceptions: Joining Another Party After Nomination: If a member, after being nominated as a member of the House, joins any political party within six months of assuming its seat, he/she is not liable for proceedings under the Xth schedule. So, point IV is not correct. Election as Speaker does not attract disqualification. It is because the post of Speaker is constitutional and neutral in nature, and resignation after election to this post does not imply defection. So, point I is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the grounds for disqualification and exceptions under the Anti-Defection Law.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Joint Public Service Commission:
I. An Act of the Parliament is required to establish the Joint Public Service Commission for two States.
II. The members of the Joint Public Service Commission are appointed by the President.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Article 315(2) of the Constitution of India outlines the process for establishing a Joint State Public Service Commission (JSPSC) for two or more states. JSPSC is a statutory and not a constitutional body. A JSPSC can be created by an Act of Parliament on the request of the State Legislatures concerned. The Constitution states that if two or more States agree to have a single Public Service Commission, and each State's legislature (or each House in bicameral legislatures) passes a resolution, then Parliament can legislate to establish a JPSC. So, statement I is correct. The chairman and members of a JSPSC are appointed by the President. They hold office for a term of six years or until they attain the age of 62 years, whichever is earlier. They can be suspended or removed by the President. So, statement II is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the establishment, appointment, and removal process of the JSPSC.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Finance Commission:
I. It functions as a quasi-judicial body.
II. It recommends the measures needed to augment the Consolidated Fund of a State to supplement the resources of the Panchayats in the State.
III. Its recommendations should be laid before the Parliament with an explanatory memorandum as to the action taken thereon.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Finance Commission is a constitutional body under Article 280 of the Constitution of India. The Commission has quasi-judicial functions. Despite not being a court, the Finance Commission's work involves assessing financial needs, balancing the competing claims of different states, and applying objective criteria in its recommendations. So, statement I is correct. It shall be the duty of the Commission to make recommendations to the President as to: the measures needed to augment the Consolidated Fund of a State to supplement the resources of the Panchayats in the State on the basis of the recommendations made by the Finance Commission of the State. So, statement II is correct. As per Article 281, the President shall cause every recommendation made by the Finance Commission under the provisions of this Constitution together with an explanatory memorandum as to the action taken thereon to be laid before each House of Parliament. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the functions, duties, and reporting mechanism of the Finance Commission.

questionText: Which one of the following statements is not correct?
options: (a) The President can notify castes, races, or tribes to be considered as Scheduled Castes.
(b) Only the President can notify any caste to be recognised as Socially and Educationally Backward classes for the purpose of State as well as Central list.
(c) The State Government can provide for the sub-classification of Scheduled Castes.
(d) The opinion of the National Commission Scheduled Tribes is not binding for the recognition of Scheduled-Tribes.
explanation: According to the Constitution under Article 341, the President has the authority to specify castes as Scheduled Castes. The President, through public notification, can specify which castes, races, or tribes are to be considered SCs in relation to a particular state or union territory, after consultation with the Governor. The Parliament can then make additions or deletions to the list of SCs specified by the President. Article 342A, as amended latest by the 105th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2021 specifies that, State or Union territory may, by law, prepare and maintain, for its own purposes, a list of socially and educationally backward classes, entries in which may be different from the Central List. In a recent seven judge bench judgement the, the Supreme Court allowed the State Governments to sub-classify the Scheduled Caste list for the targeted application of reservation policies. Further, it said that the State Governments can sub-classify the SC list on its own as it doesn't involve inclusion or exclusion from the list which may have required Parliamentary approval. While recognizing any class of persons as a Scheduled Tribe, though it is mandated to render the advice of the National Commission of Scheduled Tribes and the Registrar General of India, the recommendations of neither of them are binding on the President.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Detailed knowledge of the constitutional provisions and recent Supreme Court judgments regarding the specification and sub-classification of SCs, STs, and OBCs.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the recognition of minorities in India:
I. The recognition of linguistic minorities in India is done State wise, not nationally.
II. At present, the religious minorities are identified at the national level.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Article 30 of the Constitution of India talks about only two kinds of minorities, that is, religious and linguistic minorities. Linguistic minorities in India refer to groups or communities residing in any part of the country who have a distinct language or script of their own. Although the Constitution does not provide a precise definition of 'linguistic minorities', it is well established that such groups need not necessarily speak one of the 22 languages listed in the Eighth Schedule. The Supreme Court in TMA Pai foundation versus the State of Karnataka, 2002 case stated that the determination of Linguistic Minorities is to be done State wise, not nationally. So, statement I is correct. The Union Government set up the National Commission for Minorities (NCM) under the National Commission for Minorities Act, 1992. Initially five religious communities, viz., Muslims, Christians, Sikhs, Buddhists and Zoroastrians (Parsis) were notified as minority communities by the Union Government. Further vide notification 2014, Jains were also notified as another minority community. So, statement II is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the definition and determination of minorities in India, and factual knowledge of notified minority communities.

questionText: Which one of the following Constitutional Amendments gave Constitutional status to the National Commission of Scheduled Castes?
explanation: The 89th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003 established the National Commission for Scheduled Tribes (NCST) as a separate constitutional body by bifurcating the previously combined National Commission for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes. This amendment inserted Article 338A into the Constitution, exclusively dealing with the NCST. Before the amendment, a single commission was responsible for both Scheduled Castes (SCs) and Scheduled Tribes (STs). The bifurcation created two distinct bodies: the National Commission for Scheduled Castes (under Article 338) and the National Commission for Scheduled Tribes (under Article 338A). The Commission comprises a Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson, and three other Members, all of whom are appointed by the President of India.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the constitutional amendment that established the NCST and its composition.

questionText:
Consider the following:
I. Union Public Service Commission
II. Central Vigilance Commission
III. National Commission for Scheduled Castes
IV. National Human Rights Commission
How many of the above submit annual reports to the President of India?
explanation: Article 323 of the Constitution of India pertains to reports of Public Service Commissions. According to article 323, the Union Public Service Commission (UPSC) must present an annual report to the President, who then causes it to be laid before each House of Parliament along with a memorandum explaining any non-acceptance of the Commission's advice. So, point I is correct. As per the Central Vigilance Commission Act, 2003, the Central Vigilance Commission (CVC) is required to submit an annual report to the President. The President then causes it to be laid before both Houses of Parliament. So, point II is correct. According to Article 338(5)(d) of the Constitution, the National Commission for Scheduled Castes (NCSC) must submit an annual report to the President. The President then lays it before Parliament. So, point III is correct. The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC), under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993, submits its annual report to the Central Government. The Central Government then lays it before Parliament along with an action-taken memorandum. So, point IV is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the reporting mechanisms of various constitutional and statutory bodies.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. The term ‘office of profit’ is not defined anywhere in the Constitution.
II. The Parliament by law can exclude an office from the purview of ‘office of profit’.
III. The Presiding Officer of the House can disqualify any member on account of holding an office of profit.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Under the provisions of Article 102 (1) and Article 191 (1) of the Constitution of India, a Member of Parliament or a Member of State Legislature is barred from holding any office of profit under the Central or State Government. Although mentioned explicitly in the Constitution, the term is not defined anywhere in it nor it is defined in the Representation of People's Act, 1952. So, statement I is correct. Provisions of Articles 102 and 191 also protect a legislator occupying a government position if the office in question has been made immune to disqualification by law, that is, the Parliament or the State legislature can declare which of the offices will not be deemed or classified as 'office of profit' for the purpose of Article 102 and Article 191. So, statement II is correct. According to Article 103 of the Constitution, if any question arises as to whether a member of either House of Parliament has become subject to any of the disqualifications mentioned in Article 102(1), then the question shall be referred to the President whose decision in the matter would be final. Also, before giving any decision on the matter the President shall obtain the opinion of the Election Commission and shall act in accordance with such opinion. So, statement III is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the 'office of profit' provision and the process of disqualification.

questionText: If a political party has to be recognized as a National Party, then which one of the following criteria must be fulfilled by it?
options: (a) It must be ‘recognized’ in two States as a State party
(b) Its candidates should poll at least 3% of total valid votes in four or more States in the last Assembly elections
(c) It must secure 6% of the valid votes in two States where it is not recognized as a State party
(d) It must win at least 2% of the total seats in the Lok Sabha from not less than three States
explanation: Election Symbols (Reservation and Allotment) Order 1968 under the Representation of People's Act, 1951 governs the recognition of political parties at the National and the State level. For a political party to be recognized as a National Party, it should fulfil any of the following conditions: a) It is 'recognized' in four or more states as a State party; or b) If its candidates polled at least 6% of total valid votes in any four or more States in the last Lok Sabha or Assembly elections and has at least four MPs in the last Lok Sabha polls; or c) If it has won at least 2% of the total seats in the Lok Sabha from not less than three States.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the criteria for recognizing a political party as a National Party.

questionText: The Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) of India acts as the "friend, philosopher, and guide" to the:
explanation: The Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) of India is an autonomous Constitutional authority tasked with auditing the financial accounts of the Government of India and its various agencies. Additionally, the CAG evaluates the government's adherence to the Constitution and other relevant laws. As an independent office the CAG is responsible to the Parliament only, to which it lays its report to, the office of the CAG is taken to as an agent of the Parliament which conducts audits of expenditure on its behalf. The CAG is a critical pillar of India's democratic system of accountability. Its key contributions include: Agent of the Parliament: The CAG conducts audits on behalf of the Parliament and is responsible to it. Link with Public Accounts Committee (PAC): The CAG acts as a 'friend, philosopher, and guide' to the PAC of the Parliament. The PAC examines the audit reports of the CAG, which forms the basis of its scrutiny of government spending. Deterrent Against Mismanagement: The CAG's audit reports highlight irregularities, wasteful expenditure and financial mismanagement, acting as a deterrent against corruption and inefficiency. By bringing financial discrepancies to the notice of the legislature, it ensures that the executive is held accountable.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the role and responsibilities of the CAG.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. Any person who is disqualified from the House on the charges of defection cannot be appointed as a Minister.
II. Any person who has been disqualified on the basis of defection cannot contest re-election for the next five years.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Anti-Defection Law, introduced through the Tenth Schedule by the 52nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1985, seeks to curb political defections that undermine democratic stability. Any person who is disqualified from the House on the charges of defection cannot be appointed as a minister in the same legislature. This is a provision of the 91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003 to the Constitution of India, which was enacted to strengthen the anti-defection law.
