Articles 75(1B) and 164(1B) (for Union and State respectively), which state that a disqualified member cannot become a minister from the date of disqualification until the end of the term of the legislature. Similarly, Article 361B bars them from holding any remunerative political post during this period. So, statement I is correct. The Supreme Court in Shrimanth Balasaheb Patil vs Hon'ble Speaker Karnataka Legislative Assembly has clarified that disqualification under the Tenth Schedule does not create a ban on contesting elections. This means a member disqualified for defection can recontest and return to the legislature through a fresh mandate. So, statement II is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the implications of disqualification under the Anti-Defection Law.

questionText:
Consider the following:
I. Misbehavior
II. Incapacity
III. Insolvency
How many of the above are constitutional grounds for the impeachment of the President of India?
explanation: Article 61 of the Constitution of India describes the conditions and procedure for the removal of the incumbent President of India from its office. As per the Article, the President can be removed from office by a process of impeachment for 'violation of the Constitution'. However, the Constitution does not define the meaning of the phrase 'violation of the constitution'. Hence, the only constitutional ground for the removal of the President is the 'violation of the constitution'. So, points I, II and III are not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the sole constitutional ground for the impeachment of the President.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Delimitation Commission:
I. It is an executive body appointed by the Election Commission of India.
II. Its orders cannot be challenged in any court on any ground.
III. It was last constituted in 2002 under the Delimitation Act, 2002.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Delimitation is the act of redrawing the boundaries of Lok Sabha and State Assembly constituencies to represent changes in population. Delimitation is constitutionally mandated under Articles 82 and 170 of the Indian Constitution. Delimitation is carried out by an independent statutory body called the Delimitation Commission. The Delimitation Commission is appointed by the President of India and works in collaboration with the Election Commission of India. It is composed of the following: a retired judge of the Supreme Court or a High Court, the Chief Election Commissioner of India and the respective State Election Commissioners. So, statement I is not correct. The Delimitation Commission in India is a high power body whose orders have the force of law. Once published, the Commission's orders are final and, as per the Delimitation Commission Act 1952 and Article 329A of the Constitution, has the 'full force of law and shall not be called into question in any court'. The Supreme Court in its judgement in Kishorchandra Chhanganlal Rathod vs. Union of India, 2024 held that the constitutional courts can review the orders of the delimitation commission and can also grant appropriate remedy if an order is manifestly arbitrary and irreconcilable to constitutional values. So, statement II is not correct. Delimitation Commission is set up after the passage of a Delimitation Act by parliament from time to time. Four commissions have been established till date in 1952, 1962, 1972 and 2002. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Detailed knowledge of the composition, powers, and judicial reviewability of the Delimitation Commission.

questionText:
Consider the following:
I. The President of India
II. Attorney General for India
III. The nominated members of the Rajya Sabha
IV. The elected members of the Lok Sabha
How many of the above vote on the resolution for the removal of the Vice-President of India from office?
explanation: Article 67 of the Constitution of India makes provision regarding tenure and removal of Vice-President of India. The Vice-President may be removed from his office by a resolution of the Council of States (Rajya Sabha). Such a resolution must be passed by a majority of all the then members of the Rajya Sabha. Thus, both nominated and elected members of Rajya Sabha participate in the removal process. So, point III is correct. Such a resolution then needs to be agreed to by the House of the People (Lok Sabha). That means it is to be passed by elected members of Lok Sabha by a simple majority. So, point IV is correct. The Attorney General of India does not participate in the removal of the Vice President. So, point II is not correct. The resolution for the removal of Vice-President just like that of the Speaker of Lok Sabha, post its passage from both the Houses is not sent to the President for assent, the President is just communicated on the same. So, point I is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the procedure and participants in the removal of the Vice-President.

questionText:
With reference to the election of the Members of the Rajya Sabha, consider the following statements:
I. The election is conducted on the basis of First Past the Post (FPTP) system.
II. Members from Union Territories are indirectly elected by an electoral college.
III. The Rajya Sabha members are elected through an open ballot system.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Rajya Sabha is the Upper House of the Indian Parliament. The representatives of States in the Rajya Sabha are elected by the elected members of State Legislative Assemblies. The election is held in accordance with the system of proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote. So, statement I is not correct. The representatives of each Union Territory in the Rajya Sabha are indirectly elected by members of an electoral college specially constituted for the purpose. This election is also held in accordance with the system of proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote. So, statement II is correct. The Rajya Sabha elections are presently conducted by open ballot, prior to 2001 these elections were conducted via a secret ballot, later the Central Government passed an amendment to facilitate open ballot voting, which was subsequently upheld by the Supreme Court in Kuldip Nayar versus the Union of India case. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the election process for Rajya Sabha members from States and Union Territories.

questionText: Which one of the following would preside over the Joint Sitting of the Parliament in case both Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the Lok Sabha are absent?
options: (b) Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha
explanation: A joint sitting of both Houses is convened by the President under Article 108 of the Constitution of India. It is convened to resolve the deadlock between the two Houses over a bill. The Speaker of Lok Sabha presides over a joint sitting of the two Houses and the Deputy Speaker, in his absence. If the Deputy Speaker is also absent from a joint sitting, the Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha presides. If he is also absent, such other person as may be determined by the members present at the joint sitting, presides over the meeting. The Chairman of Rajya Sabha does not preside over a joint sitting as he is not a member of either House of Parliament.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the presiding officers for a joint sitting of Parliament.

questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct with regard to the Leader of the Opposition in Lok Sabha?
I. It is constitutionally mandatory to appoint the Leader of the Opposition.
II. He is ranked equal to the Union Cabinet Ministers in the Order of Precedence.
III. He acts as the Chief Whip for all the opposition parties.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation: In each House of the Parliament, there are provisions for the Leader of the Opposition (LoP). The position of Leader of Opposition was officially described in The Salary and Allowances of Leaders of Opposition in Parliament Act, 1977. The Constitution neither mentions about the post of LoP, nor puts an obligation on the House to create or appoint to the office. So, statement I is not correct. The LoPs are also entitled to the salary, allowances and other facilities equivalent to that of a Cabinet Minister. They are ranked equivalent to the Union Cabinet Ministers in the Order of Precedence. So, statement II is correct. Though LoP is considered as a joint leader of the opposition benches, which may have members from various Political Parties, he is not an authority over the members of the opposition benches in the official capacity. Appointing whips is an internal matter of the Parties, and each party having representation in the House can appoint any of its members as a whip. So, statement III is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the statutory basis, status, and authority of the Leader of the Opposition.

questionText:
Consider the following functionaries:
I. Chairperson of the National Council for Transgender Persons
II. Chairperson of the National Commission for Protection of Child Rights
III. Chairperson of the National Commission for Safai Karamcharis
IV. Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities
How many of the above serve as the ex-officio Members of the National Human Rights Commission (NHRC)?
explanation: The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) is an independent multi-member statutory body, first established in the year 1993, under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993. The NHRC consists of a chairperson and five full time members and seven ex-officio Members. Seven Ex-officio Members: In addition to these full-time members, the commission includes seven ex-officio members, comprising the chairpersons of various national commissions, including: The National Commission for Minorities, The National Commission for Scheduled Castes (SCs), The National Commission for Scheduled Tribes (STs), The National Commission for Women, The National Commission for Backward Classes (BCs), The National Commission for Protection of Child Rights (NCPCR). So, point II is correct. The Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities (CCPwD). So, point IV is correct. The Chairpersons of the NCTP and the NCSK are not ex officio members of the NHRC. So, point I and point III are not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the ex-officio members of the NHRC.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. All the members of All India Services work at the pleasure of the President.
II. Any member of the All India Services cannot be removed from service by any authority lower than the one who appoints them.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Part XIV of the Constitution of India contains provisions for regulating the employment of persons to the public services under the Union and the States. The Doctrine of Pleasure is an instrument of public policy which states that any servant to the sovereign (herein the President of India) serves at the pleasure of the President. Article 310 of the Constitution states that all the civil servants of the Union (members of the Defence Services, Civil Services, All-India Services or persons holding military posts or civil posts under the Centre/State) hold office at the pleasure of the President. So, statement I is correct. According to Article 311 of the Constitution, a member of an All India Service cannot be removed from their office by an authority that is lower in rank than the one that appointed them. This provision protects civil servants from arbitrary dismissal and ensures they are given a fair hearing before any disciplinary action is taken. So, statement II is correct.
subject: Polity
topic: Services under the Union and the States
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the Doctrine of Pleasure and the constitutional safeguards for civil servants.

questionText:
Which of the following statements are correct with regard to Money Bills in India?
I. It can be introduced only by a Minister and not by any other member of the Lok Sabha.
II. It can neither be rejected nor amended in the Rajya Sabha.
III. The Speaker has the final authority in deciding whether a Bill is a Money Bill or not.
IV. The President cannot withhold her assent to a Money Bill.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation: The Constitution of India provides that a bill will be deemed as Money Bill if it only deals with any or all of the matters mentioned in Article 110. Money bills can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha and only with the recommendation of the President. Money bills are considered government bills and can only be introduced by a minister. So, statement I is correct. The Rajya Sabha cannot initiate, reject, or amend money bills. It can only make the recommendations. It must return the bill to the Lok Sabha within 14 days, whether with or without recommendations. The Lok Sabha can either accept or reject all or any of the recommendations of the Rajya Sabha. So, statement II is correct. If any question arises whether a Bill is a Money Bill or not, the decision of the Speaker of the House of the People thereon shall be final. However, the Supreme Court in the Aadhar judgement of 2018 held that the Speaker's decision on whether a Bill is a money Bill or not, despite being 'final' according to the Constitution, can still be subject to judicial review. So, statement III is correct. The President may either give or withhold his assent to a Money Bill. A Money Bill can not be returned to the House by the President for reconsideration. So, statement IV is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Detailed knowledge of the legislative procedure for Money Bills and related Supreme Court judgments.

questionText:
Which of the following provisions was/were added by the 91st Constitutional Amendment Act of 2003?
I. No disqualification proceedings if one third members of a party split to form a separate group.
II. The total number of Ministers at the Union level shall not exceed 15 percent of the strength of the Lok Sabha.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation: The 91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003 was enacted to strengthen the provisions of the Anti-Defection Law and to curb the proliferation of ministerial posts. Initially, as per the provision of the Tenth Schedule, there was no disqualification of members if one third members of a party split to form a separate group. The 91st amendment deleted this provision pertaining to exemption from disqualification on the ground of splits. However, if two-thirds of a legislative party agree to merge with another party, it's considered a valid merger, and the members involved are not subject to disqualification. So, statement I is not correct. The total number of ministers, including the Prime Minister, in the Central Council of Ministers shall not exceed 15% of the total strength of the Lok Sabha. So, statement II is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the provisions introduced by the 91st Constitutional Amendment Act.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Election Commission of India(ECI):
I. The Representation of People’s Act 1951 explicitly authorises the ECI to de-register a political party.
II. Scheduling of the elections to both Central and State Legislatures is an exclusive prerogative of the ECI.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Election Commission of India (ECI) is a permanent and independent constitutional body. The ECI has the power to register political parties under Section 29A of the RPA, 1951. However, it does not have the power to de-register a political party. The Supreme Court has held that such power is not explicitly conferred on the ECI. De-registration can only happen under very limited circumstances (e.g., a party ceases to exist, or voluntarily seeks de-registration). So, statement I is not correct. The scheduling of elections to the Lok Sabha, State Legislative Assemblies, and Rajya Sabha is an exclusive prerogative of the Election Commission of India (ECI) under Article 324 of the Constitution. This includes announcing the election calendar, determining polling dates, and enforcing the Model Code of Conduct (MCC) to ensure free and fair elections. So, statement II is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the powers of the Election Commission regarding party registration and election scheduling.

questionText: Which one of the following Urban Local Bodies is not established by the State Government?
explanation: The term Urban Local Government in India signifies the governance of an urban area by the people through their elected representatives. Port Trusts: These are established in port areas to manage and protect ports and provide civic amenities. They are created by an Act of the Parliament. Municipal Corporation: These are ULBs created for administration of big cities. They are established in the States by an act of the concerned State legislature. Notified Area Committee: It is created for the administration of two types of areas - a fast developing town due to industrialization, and a town which does not yet fulfil all the conditions necessary for the constitution of municipality. It is established via a notification by the State Government. Town Area Committee: It is generally set up for the administration of a small town. It is a semi-municipal authority and is entrusted with a limited number of civic functions. It is created by a separate act of a State Legislature.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the different types of Urban Local Bodies and their methods of establishment.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Union Territories (UTs):
I. The Lieutenant Governor/Administrator of an UT has the same status as the Head of State like a Governor.
II. The President can establish a High Court for a UT or put it under jurisdiction of the High Court of the adjacent State.
III. The President can make provisions for the peace, progress, and good government of specific Union territories.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Part VIII of the Constitution of India deals with the administration of Union Territories (UTs). Every UT is administered by the President acting through an administrator appointed by him. An administrator of a Union Territory is an agent of the President and not head of state like a governor. So, statement I is not correct. The Parliament can establish a High Court for a Union Territory or put it under the jurisdiction of the High Court of an adjacent State. So, statement II is correct. The President may make regulations for the peace, progress and good government of the Union territory of (a) the Andaman and Nicobar Islands; (b) Lakshadweep; (c) Dadra and Nagar Haveli; (d) Daman and Diu; (e) Puducherry. Such a regulation has the effect of law as passed by the Parliament. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the constitutional provisions regarding the administration of Union Territories.

questionText:
Consider the following States:
I. Assam
II. Manipur
III. Meghalaya
IV. Nagaland
V. Tripura
In how many of the above states autonomous districts can be established under the Sixth Schedule of the Constitution?
explanation: The Constitution, under Sixth Schedule, contains special provisions for the administration of tribal areas in the four north eastern states of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram. So, only points I, III and V are correct. The tribal areas in the four states of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram have been constituted as autonomous districts. But, they do not fall outside the executive authority of the state concerned. The governor is empowered to organise and re-organise the autonomous districts. Each autonomous district has a district council consisting of 30 members, of whom four are nominated by the governor and the remaining 26 are elected on the basis of adult franchise.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the states covered under the Sixth Schedule.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. The Rajya Sabha can initiate the process of creating an All India Service by a resolution supported by two-third majority of members present and voting.
II. The creation of the All India Judicial Service does not require a Constitutional Amendment under Article 368.
III. The Constitution empowers the Parliament to regulate recruitment and conditions of service of persons appointed to All India Services.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Article 312 of the Constitution of India empowers the Rajya Sabha (Council of States) to initiate the process of creating a new All India Services by passing a resolution supported by not less than two-thirds of members present and voting. After the resolution is passed by the Rajya Sabha it is then sent to the Lok Sabha, where it needs to be passed by a simple majority. So, statement I is correct. Article 312 further states that any such resolution to create a new All India Service will not be restricted by the provisions as mentioned in the Chapter VI of Part VI or Part XI of the Constitution. For All India Judicial Services (AIJS), the 42nd Amendment Act, 1976, specifically mentioned that for the creation of such a service if any provision under Part VI of the constitution needed to be amended, it will not be taken to as an amendment under Article 368 of the constitution. So, statement II is correct. Article 312 of the Constitution confers power on Parliament to regulate recruitment and conditions of service of persons appointed to All India Services. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
topic: Services under the Union and the States
difficulty_rationale: Detailed knowledge of Article 312 and the process for creating new All India Services.

questionText: Which one of the following presents, on behalf of the President, the Presidential Reference made under Article 143 of the Constitution to the Supreme Court of India?
options: (a) The Union Minister of Law and Justice
explanation: Article 143 of the Constitution of India authorizes the president to seek the opinion of the Supreme Court in the two categories of matters: On any question of law or fact of public importance which has arisen or which is likely to arise. In this case, the Supreme Court may tender or may refuse to tender its opinion to the president. On any dispute arising out of any pre-constitution treaty, agreement, covenant, engagement, sanad or other similar instruments. In this case, the Supreme Court 'must' tender its opinion to the president. In both the cases, the opinion expressed by the Supreme Court is only advisory and not a judicial pronouncement. Hence, it is not binding on the president. The Attorney General of India represents the Government of India and presents the Presidential Reference made under Article 143 of the Constitution to the Supreme Court of India on behalf of the President. So far, the President has made sixteen references to the Supreme Court under its advisory jurisdiction.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the advisory jurisdiction of the Supreme Court under Article 143.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Union Public Service Commission (UPSC):
I. It provides advisory opinions to the Central Government in disciplinary or vigilance matters of its employees.
II. It decides the quantum and allocation of reserved seats for various posts.
III. Its jurisdiction can be extended by the President through a notification.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation: The Union Public Service Commission (UPSC) serves as the central recruiting agency of India and functions as an independent constitutional body. The UPSC offers advisory recommendations to the government, but these are not binding. The government's decisions based on UPSC's advice are open to parliamentary scrutiny. It must also be noted here, that in disciplinary or vigilance matters, the government receives advisory opinions from both UPSC and CVC. So, statement I is correct. Reservation policy (including quantum and allocation of reserved seats for SCs, STs, OBCs, EWS, etc.) is as decided by the appropriate authority. The UPSC only conducts exams and recruitment as per the reservation policy laid down by the Government. So, statement II is not correct. Under Article 321 of the Constitution, the Parliament can extend the functions (i.e., jurisdiction) of the UPSC in respect of the services of the Union or the State, through regulation. The Parliament also has the authority to exclude specific posts, services, and matters from UPSC's purview. So, statement III is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the advisory role, limitations, and extension of functions of the UPSC.

questionText:
A division is a type of voting which records how each Member of Parliament (MP) voted on a motion. With reference to division of votes, consider the following statements:
I. Any member of the Parliament can demand a division of votes on a motion.
II. It is a necessary precondition for the passage of Constitutional Amendment Acts.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: All decisions in Parliament are taken by voting by Members of Parliament (MPs). Voice voting is the preferred method of decision making by the Parliament. The rules of procedure of Lok Sabha do not mandate recording of votes of MPs for every decision taken. Voice voting does not reveal the individual positions taken by MPs. A division is a type of voting which records how each MP voted on a motion. If a member is not happy with a voice vote, it can be challenged and a division can be asked for. MPs also have the right to ask for the vote of every MP to be recorded. This is called a division. So, statement I is correct. Recording of votes is also mandated when there is a constitutional requirement for a special majority of Parliament (for example a constitutional amendment), or after a no-confidence motion. So, statement II is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the voting procedures in Parliament, including voice voting and division.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC):
I. The 100th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2018, which inserted Article 338B, gave constitutional recognition to the NCBC.
II. The President has the authority to determine the composition of the NCBC.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC) was initially a statutory body set up under the National Commission for Backward Classes Act, 1993. However, it was later given constitutional status by the 102nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 2018, which inserted Article 338B. So, statement I is not correct. While Article 338B of the Constitution of India establishes the National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC), it doesn't specify the exact number of members. Instead, it grants the President the authority to determine the composition, including the Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson, and other members, through rules. So, statement II is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the constitutional status and composition of the NCBC.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI):
I. It cannot take over any case registered in a State without the concurrence of the latter.
II. It must seek the Centre’s permission before registering a corruption case against any government servant.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) is an investigative agency under the overall purview of the Central Government It derives its authority from the Delhi Special Police Establishment (DSPE) Act, 1946. As per Section 6 of the Delhi Special Police Establishment (DSPE) Act, 1946, the CBI needs the consent from the respective State Government for conducting investigation in its jurisdiction. In addition to it, the Supreme Court and the High Courts, can order CBI to investigate such a crime anywhere in the country without the consent of the State Government. So, statement I is not correct. In 2018, the government pushed through Parliament amendments to Section 17A of the Act making it mandatory for the CBI to seek the Centre's permission before registering a case of corruption against a government servant. In January 2025, the Supreme Court ruled that the CBI does need permission to register FIR against Centre's officers posted in a different jurisdiction in a state. So, statement II is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the CBI's jurisdiction, consent requirements, and recent Supreme Court rulings.

questionText:
Consider the following:
I. Changing the allocation of Lok Sabha seats among the States.
II. Presentation of the Annual Financial Statement by a Minister other than the Finance Minister
III. Abolishing the post of the Deputy Speaker of House of the People
IV. Changing the nomenclature of the Council of States in Parliamentary proceedings
How many of the above will require an amendment to the Constitution of India?
explanation: While the seats in the Rajya Sabha are distributed amongst the States via the Fourth Schedule of the Constitution Article 82 provides that upon the completion of each census, the allocation of seats in the House of the People to the States and the division of each State into territorial constituencies shall be readjusted by such authority and in such manner as Parliament may by law determine. Thus, the Parliament by law enacts a Delimitation Act after every census. Thus, the readjustment of seats requires ordinary legislation, and not Constitutional Amendment. So, point I is not correct. Article 112 says that the President shall in respect of every financial year cause to be laid before both the Houses of Parliament the 'annual financial statement'. By convention, the Union Finance Minister presents the 'annual financial statement' or budget on behalf of the Prime Minister. The Prime Minister can allow any other Minister to present the Budget. This does not require a Constitutional Amendment. So, point II is not correct. Article 93 of Indian Constitution provides for the Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the House of the People. This post is mandated under Article 93 of the Constitution of India. Thus, abolishing this post would require amendment to Article 93. So, point III is correct. The Constitution of India refers to the Houses of Parliament as the 'Council of States' and the 'House of People'. In 1954 the Hindi names 'Rajya Sabha' and 'Lok Sabha' were adopted by the respective Houses. Hence, changing this nomenclature doesn't require any amendment to the Constitution. So, point IV is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of which actions require a constitutional amendment versus ordinary legislation or convention.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the office of Lokpal:
I. Its jurisdiction extends to the Council of Ministers including the Prime Minister.
II. Institutions partly financed by the Government of India come under its purview.
III. It can investigate Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs) receiving foreign aid.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: The Lokpal is an independent statutory body established under Section 3 of the Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013. It has jurisdiction to inquire into allegations of corruption against anyone who is or has been the Prime Minister of India, a Minister in the Union Government, or a Member of Parliament, as well as officials of the Union Government under Groups A, B, C, and D. So, statement I is correct. Institutions which are 'financed' fully or partly by the Government come under the jurisdiction of Lokpal, So, statement II is correct. The jurisdiction of Lokpal extends to the Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs) receiving foreign aid as well, the Lokpal can receive complaints and initiate inquiry against the NGOs receiving more than 10 Lakh rupees a year as foreign aid. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the jurisdiction of the Lokpal.

questionText:
Consider the following functionaries:
I. Union Home Minister
II. Leader of Opposition in Lok Sabha
III. Speaker of Lok Sabha
IV. Chief Justice of India
How many of the above are part of the selection committees for appointments to the National Human Rights Commission and the Lokpal?
explanation: The appointment of the chairperson and members of the NHRC is carried out by the President of India based on the recommendations of a six-member committee. This committee is headed by the Prime Minister and includes the following members: Speaker of the Lok Sabha, Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha, Leaders of the Opposition in both Houses of Parliament, Union Home Minister. The Chairperson and the Members of the Lokpal are appointed by the President of India on the recommendation of a selection committee composed of the Prime Minister as the Chairperson, the Speaker of Lok Sabha, the Leader of Opposition in Lok Sabha, the Chief Justice of India or a Judge nominated by him/her, and one eminent jurist. Therefore, the Leader of Opposition in Lok Sabha and the Speaker of Lok Sabha are common to both selection committees.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the composition of the selection committees for the NHRC and the Lokpal.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. The President addresses the Parliament only on the commencement of every Budget Session.
II. The Presidential address is discussed on a Motion of Thanks, which is answered by the Prime Minister.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Article 87 of the Constitution of India mandates a special address by the President of India to both Houses of Parliament. Article 87 provides for two special occasions on which the President addresses a joint sitting - First is to address the new legislature after a General Election, second is to address the first sitting of the Parliament every year. So, statement I is not correct. Under rule 15 of the Rules of Procedure, discussion on the matters referred to in the President's address takes place on a Motion of Thanks moved by a member and seconded by another member. The Motion of Thanks is moved in each House by ruling party MPs, followed by discussions that last up to three or four days and conclude with the Prime Minister replying to the points raised during the discussion. So, statement II is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the occasions for the President's special address and the procedure for the Motion of Thanks.

questionText:
Consider the following:
I. State Human Rights Commission
II. State Information Commission
III. State Public Service Commission
IV. State Election Commission
For how many of the above bodies, chairperson/ members are appointed by the Governor, but can only be removed by the President?
explanation: State Human Rights Commission (SHRC): The Governor appoints the Chairperson and members of the SHRC. However, the President of India has the authority to remove the Chairperson and members, as per Section 23 of the Act. So, point I is correct. State Information Commission (SIC): The Governor appoints the Chief Information Commissioner and other Information Commissioners of the State. The Governor has the power to remove these commissioners, as per Section 17(1) and 17(3) of the Act. So, point II is not correct. State Public Service Commission (SPSC): It is a constitutional body and has its members appointed by the Governor according to Article 316. However, the President of India has the authority to remove them, but only after the Supreme Court has conducted an inquiry into allegations of misbehaviour, as outlined in Article 317. So, point III is correct. State Election Commission (SEC): It is a constitutional body headed by a State Election Commissioner who is appointed by the Governor of the state. As per Article 243K the Commissioner can only be removed except in like manner and on the like ground as a Judge of a High Court, hence the State Election Commissioner cannot be removed by the Governor, but by the President. So, point IV is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the appointment and removal authorities for various state-level commissions.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. The State Government is empowered to dissolve an Urban Local Body before the expiry of its five-year term.
II. The Urban Local Body reconstituted after premature dissolution enjoys the full term of five years.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The 74th Constitutional Amendment Act of 1992 provided for the creation of Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) by the State Governments within the purview of Part IXA of the Constitution. As per Article 243U of the Constitution of India, an Urban Local Body (ULB) may be dissolved by the State Government before the expiry of its five-year term in accordance with state law. So, statement I is correct. A municipality constituted upon the dissolution of a municipality before the expiration of its duration shall continue only for the remainder of the period for which the dissolved municipality would have continued had it not been so dissolved. In other words, a municipality reconstituted after premature dissolution does not enjoy the full period of five years but remains in office only for the remainder of the period. So, statement II is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the dissolution and reconstitution of Urban Local Bodies under Article 243U.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. The Parliament can make laws on any matters listed in List II of the Seventh Schedule for the Union Territories (UTs).
II. In case of any inconsistency between the laws made by Parliament and those by the UT legislature, the latter prevails.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Union Territories (UTs) are the areas which are under the direct control and administration of the Central Government. For UTs with legislature as in Delhi, Puducherry and Jammu and Kashmir, some subjects from the State List have been delegated to the respective legislatures to legislate. It must be noted here that, the legislative power of Parliament for the Union Territories on subjects of the State List remain unaffected even after establishing a local legislature for them, hence the Parliament can make laws on any matters listed in List II of the Seventh Schedule for the Union Territories (UTs). So, statement I is correct. When both the Union Territory legislature and Parliament legislate on the same subject, Parliament's law prevails. In case of any inconsistency between the laws made by Parliament and those by the UT legislature, the latter becomes void to the extent of repugnancy, unless the Parliamentary law allows the UT law to stand. This ensures central supremacy over UT legislation. So, statement II is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the legislative powers of Parliament over Union Territories with legislatures.

questionText: The functioning of which one of the following committees of the Parliament is quasi-judicial in nature?
explanation: A quasi-judicial function refers to the discharge of functions by any commission established by law, administrative officers, or bodies to determine the rights of those who appear before it. Amongst the standing Parliamentary Committees, the Committee on Privileges and the Ethics Committee are said to have quasi-judicial powers. The Committee on Privileges deals with cases of breach of parliamentary privileges. As for its quasi-judicial functioning, the committee is vested with powers of: Conducting inquiries into alleged breaches of parliamentary privilege when referred by the Speaker/Chairman or the House. Examining facts, hearing explanations, and determining whether a breach has occurred. Summoning and ascribing oath: The committee can summon any person to testify (with the permission of the Presiding Officer) to testify and make him/her ascribe to an oath. Submitting recommendations to the presiding officer or House-these are not legally binding but are authoritative within parliamentary practice. Though it does not follow strict judicial procedures, its investigative and adjudicatory role ensures discipline and accountability within the Legislature. The committee also handles petitions regarding disqualification of members on grounds such as defection (if referred by the Speaker or Chairman).
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the quasi-judicial functions of the Committee on Privileges.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Central Administrative Tribunal (CAT):
I. It is a constitutional body established under Article 323A of the Constitution of India.
II. Its members have a fixed tenure of five years or hold office until the age of 60 years.
III. Any appeal against the orders of the CAT lies with the High Court.
Which of the statements given above are not correct?
explanation: Central Administrative Tribunal (CAT) is a specialized quasi-judicial body established under Article 323A of Constitution of India to adjudicate disputes related to recruitment and service conditions of public servants. The foundation of the formation of the CAT was laid down by the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976 which added Part XIVA to the Constitution. Accordingly, Administrative Tribunals Act of 1985 was passed by the Parliament, under which the CAT was institutionalized. Hence, CAT is a statutory body. So, statement I is not correct. The tribunal comprises a chairman (a sitting or retired High Court Judge) and both Administrative and Judicial Members, chosen for their expertise in relevant areas. The members have a 5 years tenure with a maximum age to serve being 65 years (68 for the Chairperson). So, statement II is not correct. The appeals against the order of the CAT are placed before the High Courts. Earlier the provisions of Article 323A provided that the appeals from tribunals established under the purview of it lie before the Supreme Court only, but the apex court in L. Chandra Kumar versus Union of India and Ors, 1997 stated that the primary appellate body for orders from CAT should be the respective High Courts. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the establishment, composition, and appellate jurisdiction regarding the Central Administrative Tribunal.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Governor of a State:
I. He can summon a session of the Legislature for the purpose of a floor test.
II. The powers to summon the State Legislature is solely vested with the Governor of the State.
III. He cannot ask for a floor test if the legislature is already in session.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Article 174 of the Constitution of India gives the Governor the power to summon from time to time 'the House or each House of the Legislature of the State to meet at such time and place as he thinks fit..' The phrase 'as he thinks fit' is read as per Article 163 of the Constitution which says the Governor acts on the aid and advice of the Cabinet. In the case of Nabam Rebia and Bamang Felix versus Deputy Speaker, 2016 clarified that 'In ordinary circumstances during the period when the Chief Minister and his Council of Ministers enjoys the confidence of the majority the House, the power vested with the Governor under Article 174 to summon, prorogue or dissolve the House is to be exercised as per the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers'. The Court further noted that 'in situations where the Government in power in opinion of the Governor has lost the confidence of the House, then it would be open to the Governor to exercise the powers vested with him under Article 174 on his own, and without any aid and advice'. So, statement I is correct. In the same case, the Court expressly said that the powers to summon the House is not solely vested with the Governor. 'When the House is in session, it is the Speaker who can call for a floor test. But when the Assembly is not in session, the Governor's residuary powers under Article 163 allow him to call for a floor test. So, statement II is not correct. However, the Governor can still recommend a floor test if there is credible evidence that the ruling government may have lost its majority. In such cases, the Governor may direct the Chief Minister to prove majority, but the actual conduct of the floor test lies within the Speaker's domain, as he controls the proceedings of the House. Hence, the Governor can still recommend a floor test when the Assembly is in session. So, statement III is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the Governor's powers to summon the House and call for a floor test, based on Supreme Court judgments.

questionText:
In which of the following cases is it mandatory for the Governor to reserve a bill for the assent of the President?
I. If a bill is violative of any of the Directive Principles of State Policy
II. If a bill is in contravention to any of the laws passed by the Parliament
III. If a bill endangers the position of the High Court
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation: Under Article 200 of the Constitution of India, when a Bill passed by the State Legislature is presented, the Governor may assent to the Bill, withhold assent, reserve it for the President's consideration, or return it (except a Money Bill) for reconsideration. If the Bill, in the Governor's opinion, undermines the constitutional position of the High Court, it must be reserved for the President under Article 200. So, point III is correct. There are also bills that the Governor may reserve for the President's assent for specific constitutional objectives. These include: Legislation seeking immunity from Articles 14 and 19-for example, laws related to the acquisition of estates under Article 31A or those intended to implement Directive Principles under Article 31C. There is no constitutional requirement to mandatorily reserve a bill that is violative of any of the Directive Principles of State Policy. So, point I is not correct. Bills on Concurrent List subjects, if inconsistent with Union laws, may be reserved under Article 254(2) to ensure their validity. The bill in contravention to any of the laws passed by the Parliament is not mandatory to get reserved for Presidential assent. So, point II is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Detailed knowledge of the circumstances under which a Governor must or may reserve a bill for the President's consideration.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. A person who is not a member of the State Legislature cannot be appointed as a Minister in the State Cabinet.
II. Ministers are appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Chief Minister, and they don’t have a security of tenure.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Articles 163 and 164 deal with the provisions of the Council of Ministers in the state. The Chief Minister is appointed by the Governor and the other Ministers are appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Chief Minister, and they shall hold office during the pleasure of the Governor, that is they don't have a security of tenure and can be removed at will of the Governor. The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the State Legislative Assembly. So, Statement II is correct. Article 164(4) of the Constitution of India allows for the appointment of a non-member of the State Legislature as a minister. However, this appointment is conditional. The individual needs to get elected/nominated to the State Legislature (either the Legislative Assembly or Legislative Council) within six months of their appointment. If they fail to do so, they cease to be a minister. So, Statement I is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the appointment, tenure, and collective responsibility of the State Council of Ministers.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Legislative Council:
I. The strength of the Legislative Council of the State is determined by the State Legislative Assembly.
II. The total number of members in the Legislative Council shall not exceed one-third of the total number of members in the Legislative Assembly of that State.
III. One-third members of the Legislative Council are elected by members of local bodies in the State.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: The Legislative Council is the Upper House (Vidhan Parishad) in the bicameral Legislature of some Indian states. The scheme of composition of a Legislative Council as laid down in the Constitution is tentative and not final. The Article 172 itself authorizes the Parliament to modify or replace the same. However, it has not enacted any such law so far. So, statement I is not correct. As per Article 171, the total number of members in the Legislative Council of a State shall not exceed one-third of the total number of members in the Legislative Assembly of that State and shall in no case be less than forty. However, the exact number of seats in a State's Legislative Council is to be determined by Parliament by law. So, Statement II is correct. Article 172 deals with the composition of the Legislative Council. In the Legislative Council: One-third members are to be elected by members of local bodies in the State. So, Statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the composition and constitutional provisions regarding the State Legislative Council.

questionText:
Consider the following:
I. Governor of the State
II. Advocate General of the State
III. Speaker of the Assembly
IV. Committees of the House
The privileges of the State Legislature extend to how many of the above?
explanation: Article 194 of the Constitution of India provides for certain Privileges to the State Legislature. Under Article 194, these privileges apply to: Members of the State Legislature, Legislative Committees. So, point IV is correct. And those participating in legislative business, such as the Advocate General. So, point II is correct. The Governor, though an integral part of the Legislature, does not enjoy these privileges, similar to how the President is excluded at the Union level. The office of the Governor has its own privileges separate from that of the State Legislature. So, point I is not correct. The Speaker is a member of the Legislative Assembly and the presiding officer of the House. The individual privileges granted to members (like freedom of speech, freedom from civil arrest, etc.) also apply to the Speaker. So, point III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the applicability of privileges of the State Legislature to various functionaries.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the State Legislature:
I. Unlike Parliament, the Constitution of India does not provide for a joint sitting of the State Legislature to resolve deadlocks between the two Houses.
II. If the State Assembly passes a bill a second time and it is rejected by the Legislative Council, it is deemed to be passed in the form originally passed by the Assembly.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Constitution of India does not provide for a joint sitting of the State Legislature (unlike Parliament) to resolve deadlocks between the two Houses. If a bill is passed by the Legislative Assembly and then rejected by the Legislative Council, the bill does not proceed to a joint sitting. Instead, the Legislative Assembly has the power to override the Council. So, statement I is correct. If the Assembly passes the bill again and sends it to the Council, and the Council either rejects it again, suggests unacceptable amendments, or fails to act within one month, then, the bill is deemed to have been passed by both Houses in the form originally passed by the Assembly the second time. So, statement II is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the procedure for resolving deadlocks in a bicameral State Legislature.

questionText:
Consider the following:
I. Establishment of a three-tier institution of Panchayats
II. Direct election to the Chairperson of the Gram Panchayat
III. Organisation of State Finance Commission after every five years
IV. Reservation for the Other Backward Classes in the Panchayats
How many of the above provisions under the Part IX of the Constitution are mandatory for the State Governments?
explanation: The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992, gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj Institutions. The Part IX contains both mandatory (compulsory) and voluntary provisions for Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs) in India. Mandatory provisions include: Three-tier Panchayati Raj Structure: Establishment of Panchayats at the village, intermediate, and district levels in all states (except those with populations below 20 lakhs). So, point I is not correct. Indirect Election of Chairpersons: Chairpersons of Panchayats at the intermediate and district levels to be elected indirectly by the elected members. While the chairperson of the intermediate and district levels must be elected indirectly, it is up to the State Legislature to decide whether to conduct elections for the Chairperson of Gram Panchayat directly or indirectly. So, point II is not correct. Reservation of Seats: For Scheduled Castes (SCs) and Scheduled Tribes (STs) in proportion to their population. One-third reservation for women, including in SC/ST categories and chairperson positions. So, point IV is not correct. State Finance Commission: Constitution of a State Finance Commission every five years to review and recommend financial distribution to Panchayats. So, point III is correct. Voluntary Provisions include Reservation for Backward Classes: Providing reservation of seats for Other Backward Classes (OBCs).
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Detailed knowledge of the mandatory versus voluntary provisions of the 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act.

questionText:
Consider the following;
I. Conduct of elections to municipality and panchayats
II. Preparation of electoral rolls
III. Handling of election disputes
Which of the above is/are the functions of the State Election Commission (SEC)?
explanation: The State Election Commission is an independent, autonomous, Constitutional body created under Article 243K of the Constitution of India. It is responsible for ensuring free, fair, and unbiased elections to local bodies in the state, such as Panchayats and Municipalities. So, point I is correct. The power of superintendence, direction, and control over the preparation of electoral rolls and the conduct of elections to the Panchayats lies with the State Election Commission, which comprises a State Election Commissioner appointed by the Governor. So, point II is correct. The State Election Commissioner can only be removed in the same manner and on the same grounds as a High Court Judge, that is it can only be removed by the President post the motion of its removal is passed by two-thirds majority in the Parliament. The State Governor or the Legislature has no role to play in the removal of a State Election Commissioner. The role of State Election Commissioner and the State Election Commission is restricted to delimitation, conduct and declaration of the result. The disputes to the elections are to be put forth before a body as is specified by the State Legislature via legislation. So, point III is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the appointment, removal, and functions of the State Election Commission.

questionText:
With reference to the Parliament, consider the following statements:
I. The authoritative text of the bills that are presented in the Parliament shall be in English.
II. The language for the transaction of business in the Parliament shall be either Hindi or English.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Article 348 of the Constitution of India says that the authoritative texts of all Bills to be introduced in either House of Parliament shall be in the English language. So, statement I is correct. Article 120 of the Constitution says that business in Parliament shall be transacted in Hindi or in English. The use of English was to be stopped after fifteen years from the commencement of this Constitution. But, the Official Languages Act, 1963 provided that the English language will continue to be used in addition to Hindi for all the official purposes of the Union and for the transaction of business in Parliament. So, statement II is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the constitutional provisions regarding the language to be used in Parliament and for Bills.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Law Commission of India:
I. It is a statutory body constituted by the Ministry of Law and Justice.
II. It is always chaired by a retired Chief Justice of India.
III. It is constituted after every five years.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation: Law Commission of India is a non-statutory body and is constituted by a notification of the Ministry of Law and Justice, Government of India, with a definite terms of reference to carry out research in the field of law and the Commission makes recommendations to the Government (in the form of Reports) as per its terms of reference. So, statement I is not correct. The commission is usually chaired by a retired judge of the Supreme Court or a High Court, and has legal scholars as members. Serving judges can also be appointed to the commission, according to the notification on the appointment of the new panel. It is wholly an advisory body; thus its recommendations are not binding on the Government of India. So, statement II is not correct. There is no fixed tenure of the Law commission, these are constituted as per the requirement and as terms of reference according to the gazette notification. So, statement III is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the status, composition, and tenure of the Law Commission of India.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. All the contracts of the Government of India are constitutionally required to be carried out in the name of the President of India.
II. The Government contracts in the name of the President enjoys immunity from the provisions of any other statute passed by the Central or State Legislature.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: As per Article 299 of the Constitution of India, all the contracts which are made under the Executive power of the Union or the State should be made in the name of the President or the respective Governor of the State. If a contract is not made under the President's or Governor's name, such a contract will not be considered as a Government contract. So, statement I is correct. Under Clause 2 of the Article, it is made clear that the President or the Governor cannot be held personally liable for the non-performance of the contract. It must be noted here that, the immunities granted to these two functionaries for not being liable to be sued in a Court of Law doesn't extend to these contracts, that is a contract entered into in the name of the President of India, cannot and will not create an immunity against the application of any statutory prescription imposing conditions on parties to an agreement, when the Government chooses to enter into a contract. In other words, the Government can sue as well as be sued in case of non-compliance of the contract, as was made clear in the Glock-Asia Pacific Limited versus the Union of India case, 2023. So, statement II is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of Article 299 and the liability of the Government in contracts.

questionText:
Consider the following with regard to National Integration Council:
I. It was set up in 1961 for combating communalism, casteism and regionalism.
II. It comprises the Prime Minister and Chief Ministers of all States and Union Territories.
III. It is mandated to meet at least twice a year.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: The National Integration Council (NIC) was set up in pursuance of the National Integration Conference in 1961 for combating communalism, casteism, regionalism etc. The NIC is a standing body to review all matters pertaining to National Integration and to make recommendations thereon. So, statement I is correct. The NIC comprised of Prime Minister as Chairperson and Union Ministers, Leaders of Opposition in Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha, Chief Ministers of all States and UTs with Legislatures, Leaders of National Political Parties, Chairpersons of National Commissions, Media Persons, Representatives of Business, Representatives of Labour, Eminent Public Figure and Women's Representatives as members. So, statement II is correct. Meetings of NIC are convened as per requirement from time to time. There is no regular or specified time interval for convening meetings of the NIC. So, statement III is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the establishment, composition, and functioning of the National Integration Council.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the District Planning Committee (DPC):
I. Its primary function is to consolidate development plans prepared by Panchayats and Municipalities in the district.
II. All DPC members are elected from among the elected members of Panchayats and Municipalities.
III. The District Commissioner acts as the ex-officio chairperson of the DPC.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: A District Planning Committee (DPC) is a body established at the district level in every state in India, as per Article 243ZD of the Constitution. Its primary function is to consolidate development plans prepared by Panchayats (rural local governments) and Municipalities (urban local governments) within the district, and to formulate a comprehensive district development plan. So, statement I is correct. Its composition is determined by the State Legislature through law, but the Constitution provides certain minimum criteria. Article 243ZD mandates that at least four-fifths (80%) of DPC members must be elected from among the elected members of Panchayats and Municipalities, in proportion to the rural and urban population. The election of the DPC is conducted as per the procedure laid down by the State Legislature, generally overseen by the State Election Commission. Only elected members of Panchayats and Municipalities participate in the election of DPC members. Nominated members and officials do not participate. So, statement II is not correct. The Chairperson of the DPC is chosen as per the provisions of the State Law. So, statement III is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Detailed knowledge of the constitutional provisions regarding the District Planning Committee under Article 243ZD.

questionText: Which one of the following committees recommended for the Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs) to have compulsory powers of taxation?
explanation: The Ashok Mehta Committee was appointed by the Central Government in 1977 to examine the weaknesses in the functioning of the Panchayati Raj system and suggest measures for its revitalisation. One of the major themes it discussed was the financial autonomy of the Panchayats in this line it recommended for devolution of taxation powers to Panchayati Raj Institutions. In the 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act of 1993, the provision of taxation was given to Panchayats, but it is a voluntary provision. It is up to the States to grant power to Panchayats for taxation or not. Some of the key recommendations of the committee were: Two-Tier System: The traditional three-tier structure was to be replaced with a two-tier system and establish Zila Parishad at the district level as the main executive body with Mandal Panchayat below it. Empowerment of Zila Parishad: The Zila Parishad was to function as the executive body. Financial Autonomy: PRIs should be granted compulsory powers of taxation. Social Justice and Oversight: A system of regular social audits. Reservation of seats for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes. Independent Judiciary at Grassroots: Nyaya Panchayats were to be constituted as separate entities. Election Reforms: Panchayat elections to be conducted by the Chief Electoral Officer of the state. Administrative and Ministerial Support: A Minister for Panchayati Raj should be appointed in the State Council of Ministers. Constitutional Backing: Recommended that Panchayati Raj Institutions be accorded constitutional status.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the recommendations of the Ashok Mehta Committee on Panchayati Raj.

questionText:
Which of the following bills/resolutions will not lapse on the dissolution of the Lok Sabha?
I. A bill for which the President has notified a joint sitting of both the Houses
II. A bill passed by the Lok Sabha but pending in the Rajya Sabha
III. A bill returned by the President for the reconsideration
IV. A resolution for the removal of a High Court judge pending in the Lok Sabha
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation: Dissolution of the House means the end of the life of the Lok Sabha either by an order made by the President under Article 85 (2) (b) of the Constitution or on the expiration of the period of five years from the date appointed for its first meeting. The position with respect to lapsing of bills on the dissolution of the Lok Sabha is as follows: A bill pending in the Lok Sabha lapses. A bill not passed by the two Houses due to disagreement and if the president has notified the holding of a joint sitting before the dissolution of Lok Sabha, does not lapse. So, point I is correct. A bill passed by the Lok Sabha but pending in the Rajya Sabha lapses. So, point II is not correct. A bill pending in the Rajya Sabha but not passed by the Lok Sabha does not lapse. A bill passed by both Houses but pending assent of the president does not lapse. A bill passed by both Houses but returned by the president for reconsideration of Houses does not lapse. So, point III is correct. A removal motion for the removal of a judge does not lapse on the dissolution of the Lok Sabha. So, point IV is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the rules regarding the lapsing of bills upon the dissolution of the Lok Sabha.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Scheduled Areas:
I. Their boundaries are determined by the Parliament.
II. The Governor can decide whether or not a law of the Parliament applies to the area.
III. The decrees of the High Court apply to the Scheduled Area like other parts of the State.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Fifth Schedule of the Constitution of India deals with the administration and control of Scheduled areas and Scheduled tribes in any state except the four states of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram. The President is empowered to declare any area to be a scheduled area. He can also increase or decrease its area, alter its boundary lines, rescind such designation or make fresh orders for such designation on an area in consultation with the Governor of the State concerned. So, statement I is not correct. The Governor of the respective State is empowered to direct that any particular law enacted by Parliament or the State Legislature shall not apply to a Scheduled Area or shall apply with specified modifications or exceptions. So, statement II is correct. Scheduled Areas are part of the respective states, and the jurisdiction of the High Court extends to all parts of the State, including Scheduled Areas. There is no constitutional bar on the applicability of High Court decrees to Scheduled Areas. So, statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the provisions of the Fifth Schedule regarding the administration of Scheduled Areas.

questionText:
Consider the following pairs in the context of Panchayati Raj Institutions:
Committee Recommendation
I Balwant Rai Mehta Committee Democratic decentralisation
II. G.V.K. Rao Committee Constitutional recognition to Panchayati Raj
III. L M Singhvi Committee District is the proper unit for planning and development
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
explanation: Balwant Rai Mehta Committee: In January 1957, the Government of India appointed a committee to examine the working of the Community Development Programme (1952) and the National Extension Service (1953) and to suggest measures for their better working. The committee submitted its report in November 1957 and recommended the establishment of the scheme of 'democratic decentralisation', which ultimately came to be known as Panchayati Raj. So, pair I is correctly matched. G.V.K. Rao Committee: The Committee to review the existing Administrative Arrangements for Rural Development and Poverty Alleviation Programmes under the chairmanship of G.V.K. Rao was appointed by the Planning Commission in 1985. The Committee recommended that the district level body, that is, the Zila Parishad should be of pivotal importance in the scheme of democratic decentralisation. It stated that 'the district is the proper unit for planning and development and the Zila Parishad should become the principal body for management of all development programmes which can be handled at that level. So, pair II is not correctly matched. L M Singhvi Committee: In 1986, Rajiv Gandhi government appointed a committee to prepare a concept paper on 'Revitalisation of Panchayati Raj Institutions for Democracy and Development' under the chairmanship of L.M. Singhvi. The committee recommended that the Panchayati Raj institutions should be constitutionally recognised, protected and preserved. So, pair III is not correctly matched.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the various committees on Panchayati Raj and their key recommendations.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. The Parliamentary Committees are constituted by the President on the advice of the Prime Minister.
II. The chairperson of all the Parliamentary committees is appointed by the respective Presiding officer.
III. The tenure of all Parliamentary Committees is concurrent with that of the Lok Sabha.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
explanation: Parliamentary Committees play a crucial role in ensuring government accountability by reviewing its policies and programmes and recommending improvements. A Parliamentary Committee consists of Members of Parliament (MPs) who are either elected or appointed from among the members of the House or nominated by the Presiding Officers of the respective Houses. The commissioning of a committee is an internal business of the Parliament, and the President in its official capacity has no role to play, rather it is the Speaker or the Chairman which appoints the members of the committees. So, statement I is not correct. The Chairperson of each of the Parliamentary Committees is appointed by the Chairman, Rajya Sabha and by the Speaker in Lok Sabha from amongst the members of the Committee. So, Statement II is correct. Parliamentary Committees in India are broadly classified into two types: Standing Committees and Ad hoc Committees. Standing Committees are permanent in nature and are constituted by Parliament to focus on specific areas of public policy or administration. In contrast, Ad hoc Committees are temporary bodies set up for a specific purpose and a limited time frame. The Standing Committees are continuous bodies that don't dissolve with the dissolution of the Lok Sabha, rather it is just their members who are changed. Hence, neither the ad hoc or the standing committees have their tenure co-terminus with the Lok Sabha. So, statement III is not correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Conceptual understanding of the types, appointment, and tenure of Parliamentary Committees.

questionText: Which one of the following statements is not correct with regard to the legislative powers of the Governor?
options: (a) The Governor cannot give assent to a bill the second time, if the same was reserved for the President at the first instance.
(b) A money bill cannot be introduced in the State Legislature without prior recommendation of the Governor.
(c) It is a constitutional responsibility of the Governor to address the State Legislature at the first sitting of the year.
(d) The veto powers of the Governor are concurrent with that of the President.
explanation: Chapter IV of the Part VI of the Constitution of India provides for the legislative powers of the Governor in relation to the State Legislature. These powers include: Powers to summon, prorogue and dissolve the Legislative Assembly, Sending messages to the Legislature, Nominating 1/6th members to the Legislative Council and 1 Anglo-Indian (if needed) to the Assembly, Providing assent to the bills passed by the Legislature, withholding, returning (if not a money bill), or reserving for the President, Promulgating ordinances when Legislature is not in session. Article 111 of the Constitution of India, provides for certain vetoing powers to the President. While in the case of the Governor he/she can either grant assent, withhold or reserve the bill for the President. At the maximum it can reserve the bill for the consideration of the President. Hence, the President has much broader vetoing powers than the Governor. As per Article 201 of the Constitution, when the Governor reserves a bill for the consideration of the President, he will not have any further role in the enactment of the bill. If the bill is returned by the President for the reconsideration of the House or Houses and is passed again, the bill must be presented again for the Presidential assent only. If the President gives his assent to the bill, it becomes an Act. This meant that the assent of the Governor was no longer needed. Like Parliament, a Money Bill cannot be introduced in a State Legislature without the prior recommendation of the Governor as per Article 207(1). As per Article 176, the Governor must address the State Legislature at the beginning of the first session every year and after each general election, and it is the Constitutional responsibility of the office of the Governor.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Comprehensive knowledge of the legislative powers of the Governor and the comparison with the President's powers.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Public Accounts Committee (PAC):
I. Its members are elected by both Houses of Parliament through proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote.
II. Ministers are prohibited from being members of the PAC.
III. Each member serves a term of one year, after which the committee is reconstituted.
Select the correct answer from the codes given below:
explanation: The Public Accounts Committee (PAC) is one of the three standing financial committees of the Parliament. It examines the government's revenue and expenditure, ensuring public funds are spent efficiently and legally. It reviews audit reports from the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG), investigating financial irregularities and recommending corrective measures. It currently comprises 22 members, 15 from the Lok Sabha, and 7 from the Rajya Sabha. The members are elected annually by both Houses of Parliament through proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote, ensuring equitable representation of political parties. So, Statement I is correct. Ministers are prohibited from being members of the PAC, a rule designed to prevent executive influence over legislative review. This safeguard helps maintain the committee's independence and objectivity in examining government expenditure and financial administration. So, Statement II is correct. The term of office for PAC members is one year. Each member serves a term of one year, after which the committee is reconstituted. Despite the brief tenure, the PAC ensures continuity and institutional expertise through the reappointment of members and consistent support from the parliamentary secretariat and CAG audit reports. So, Statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the composition, election method, and tenure of the Public Accounts Committee.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. Not less than one-third of the total number of seats to be filled by direct election at all levels of Panchayats are reserved for women.
II. Reservation for the Scheduled Castes in the Panchayats are in proportion to their population in the Panchayat area.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Article 243D of the Constitution of India deals with reservations of seats and offices in Panchayati Raj Institutions to ensure representation of historically marginalised groups, namely Scheduled Castes (SCs), Scheduled Tribes (STs), and women. Seats are to be reserved for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in every Panchayat (village, intermediate, and district levels), in proportion to their population in that Panchayat area. So, statement II is correct. As per Part IX of the Constitution, not less than one-third of the total number of seats at all levels should be reserved for women. It must be noted that there is no maximum ceiling on the quantum of reservation to women in Panchayats. So, statement I is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Factual knowledge of the reservation provisions for SCs, STs, and women in Panchayati Raj Institutions under Article 243D.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Panchayat Extension to Scheduled Areas (PESA) Act, 1996:
I. The post of chairperson of the Panchayat is reserved for Scheduled Tribes.
II. The ownership of the Minor Forest Produce lies with the Panchayats.
III. Recommendation of the Gram Sabha or Panchayat is mandatory before any land acquisition.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: The Panchayats (Extension to the Scheduled Areas) Act 1996 was enacted to extend the provisions of Part IX of the constitution to the Fifth Schedule Areas with certain modifications. It was aimed at providing greater autonomy to the Scheduled areas, which are predominantly tribal in nature, and protect their rights over land and forest. Unlike Panchayats constituted elsewhere in India, for the Panchayats under PESA no less than 50% of the total seats in every Panchayat are to be reserved for Scheduled Tribes and all Chairperson positions across all Panchayat levels are reserved for STs. So, Statement I is correct. The Panchayats under PESA also exercise ownership over minor forest produce, enabling tribal economic empowerment. So, Statement II is correct. Mandatory recommendation of Gram Sabha or Panchayat is needed for granting mining leases or concessions for minor minerals by auction and also before land acquisition or resettlement in Scheduled Areas. So, Statement III is correct.
subject: Polity
difficulty_rationale: Detailed knowledge of the specific provisions and powers granted under the PESA Act, 1996.
